title: Comment from Willis, Debi, HHS_FRDOC_0001, HHS_FRDOC_0001-1013, 2025-23896
comment_text: I request that you reconsider the proposal to remove the Consolidated CDA creation certification criterion in § 170.315(g)(6). Though FHIR may have the ability to provide discrete structured elements found in CDAs, the segmented data elements in the varying FHIR resources are not all easily and quickly located if a patient or caregiver wishes to understand "What happened at the ER visit last night?". To gather this information without a consolidated document, a user must look at timestamps for all resources to get a clear and complete picture of the events that happened recently. Additionally, if a user wishes to send the information to someone else, the consolidated document is the easiest resource (rather than researching and copying down multiple FHIR resources from a variety of locations). It is also my experience that consolidated documents contain valuable information for patients and caregivers that is not found in any other resource. The loss of this significant and valuable document will have a negative impact on patients and caregivers.

title: Comment from IRIS Health Solutions, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Introduction and Integrator Perspective

I appreciate the opportunity to comment on the HTI-5 Proposed Rule. I submit this comment from the perspective of a systems integrator that routinely translates regulatory requirements into production systems and therefore strongly support HTI-5’s shift toward outcome-driven, standards-based interoperability.

Support for Deregulatory Reset of Health IT Certification

HTI-5 appropriately recognizes that the current certification framework contains requirements that are costly to implement but low in operational value. From an integrator standpoint, we consistently observe that:
•Certification artifacts lag real-world technical practice
•Compliance effort displaces investment in reliability and scalability
•Redundant criteria do not materially improve interoperability

In production environments, interoperability success depends on API availability, data normalization, security, and operational governance—not static documentation. Removing low-value certification criteria allows vendors and integrators to focus on interoperability that works at scale, particularly for government programs operating under constrained budgets and timelines.

API-First Interoperability and Integration Layers

HTI-5’s emphasis on FHIR-based APIs aligns with modern integration practice. Real-world government health environments depend on integration layers, not single systems, to connect:
•HL7 v2 clinical feeds
•X12 eligibility and claims transactions
•Legacy batch and flat-file systems
•FHIR APIs for modern access

Integration platforms translate legacy formats into FHIR resources (e.g., Patient, Encounter, Coverage, Claim, Observation) while enforcing consistent security, auditing, and governance. Prescriptive system-level certification requirements often conflict with these architectures, increasing fragility and cost.

HTI-5’s outcome-oriented approach enables modular, resilient architectures that evolve alongside standards and technologies rather than being locked to static compliance models.

Information Blocking and Automated Access

HTI-5’s clarification that “access” and “use” include automated and system-to-system processes is critically important.

Many essential workflows now depend on non-human access, including:
•Eligibility and enrollment verification
•Prior authorization workflows
•Population health and quality reporting
•Public health surveillance
•AI-driven analytics

These workflows rely on APIs, event notifications, and batch or streaming pipelines—not manual user actions. Without clear regulatory alignment, contractual or technical constraints can unintentionally function as de facto information blocking.

By explicitly recognizing automated access, HTI-5 aligns policy with modern interoperability architectures and reduces ambiguity for implementers.

Enabling AI Through Data Accessibility

From a systems integrator perspective, most AI solutions operate as downstream consumers of interoperable data, not embedded components of certified EHRs. Common patterns include:
•Predictive models consuming normalized FHIR datasets
•Decision support services invoked via APIs
•Fraud and population health analytics operating in separate platforms

Reducing low-value certification requirements enables greater focus on data quality, model monitoring, and operational safety—areas that directly affect outcomes. HTI-5 appropriately prioritizes data accessibility over documentation overhead, better aligning regulation with real-world risk and benefit.

Implications for Government Health Programs

HTI-5 materially improves the feasibility of modernization initiatives across:
•Medicaid and CHIP programs
•Medicare interoperability and analytics
•Public health data modernization
•Cross-agency eligibility and benefits determination

By reducing compliance burden and clarifying automation expectations, the rule supports architectures that are more scalable, cost-effective, and compatible with cloud and hybrid deployment models.

Recommendations

We respectfully recommend that ASTP/ONC:
1.Finalize the proposed certification removals with minimal re-expansion
2.Reinforce API-first interoperability as the primary policy objective
3.Provide clear guidance affirming automated system-to-system access under information blocking rules
4.Continue engaging systems integrators during finalization and implementation

Conclusion

HTI-5 represents a necessary evolution of federal health IT policy. From the perspective of a systems integrator delivering interoperability in production environments, the rule better aligns regulation with how healthcare data is actually exchanged, secured, and used at scale.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment.

Respectfully submitted,
David Clark
HIT Consultant
IRIS Health Solutions

title: Comment from Rocha, David, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
These comments may apply more to future rulemaking:

1.CMS payer delegated entities and CMS provider delegated entities need to move to APIs.
If these delegated entities are performing delegated functions, such as delegated:
•Utilization management
•Electronic prior authorizations
•Care coordination 
•Risk adjustment
•Quality
These organizations need to be held accountable to such movement to APIs and using FHIR in production.
This is hindering CMS 0057-F to move to full enforcement when such delegated entities have not moved to APIs.

45 CFR Part 156 needs to update to include delegated entity obligations for FHIR API compliance.
New FHIR profiles should be created for delegated entities:
•DelegatedEntity
•DelegatedRelationship
•DelegatedEndpoint
If not, the value based care organizations that you requested input in the CMS RFI for the health tech ecosystem will not move to APIs at scale in record time.

2.CMS should collaborate with the Joint Commission, HL7 International, and other accrediting bodies to include auditable FHIR interoperability requirements within hospital accreditation standards.
The remaining health systems and hospitals who have not exposed live FHIR endpoints will feel the push to move to live APIs when the Joint Commission could audit them. 

3.CMS and ASTP/ONC need to monitor which state public health agencies and territorial public health agencies have live FHIR endpoints in production. 
For example, Flexpa has a list of the live state Medicaids with live FHIR endpoints.
As of 1-8-2026, there are 15 states with no live FHIR endpoints (unknown or unavailable) for their state Medicaids and 3 states that are still in process.

Reference:
https://www.flexpa.com/docs/network/directory

title: Comment from Jackson, Nursine, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Dear ASTP representatives:

I write to you as both a health care consumer and a professional nurse who has dedicated my career to advocating for safe patient care. Ensuring the integrity and security of electronic health information is vital to me professionally to ensure patient safety and personally as a consumer of the American healthcare system. While I support the goal of enhancing interoperability, I am deeply concerned that the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability (HTI-5) proposed rule risks undermining these values by modifying certification requirements before modernizing foundational data standards.  

The Critical Need for Updated Audit Trail Standards
The integrity of a medical record is only as reliable as its audit trail. An updated audit trail standard is required for the security of EHI, to verify its integrity, and to curtail billing fraud. The current standard, ASTM E2147-18, is outdated and insufficient for the modern clinical environment. Since 2018, the use and content of medical records have evolved dramatically. If we move forward with decertification or the removal of functional guardrails without first defining how data is created, collected, and preserved in a usable, searchable, and sharable format, we invite patient harm and compromise the trustworthiness of our healthcare system.  

To ensure medical records remain a "source of truth," HHS must prioritize a revision of the audit trail standard that includes:

AI, Medical Device, and Messaging Integration: Explicitly capturing the activities of AI algorithms, connected medical devices, and professional messaging to ensure clinical recommendations and data creation are transparent and traceable.

Software Identification and Interoperability: Audit trails must identify the specific software used in patient care. Furthermore, software must be interoperable for safe functionality within facilities and for sharing information in compliance with TEFCA.

Granular Metadata and Fraud Prevention: Standards must mandate the collection of precise data elements, including user roles and detailed activity logs (e.g., "copy-paste," "copy forwards," and "view-only" access). Audit trails that are transparent and reveal corrections, deletions, or overwrites are essential to prevent the fraudulent practices that cost billions of dollars annually.

Patient Access: We must address the reality that many patients still do not have access to their EHI, despite such transparency being required by law.

Preservation Over Ephemerality
The move toward interoperability requires that data be not only shareable but also durable. Data retention must be clearly defined so that information is available as long as patient care, investigations, or litigation holds are ongoing, and to remain in compliance with state and federal regulations. I strongly urge HHS to prohibit "ephemeral messaging" or any automatic destruction of data. Data must be retained in a readable, reproducible format for as long as it remains in use.

Sequential Implementation
 I agree that we must establish clear, shared definitions for terms like ePHI, EHI, Generative AI, and Synthetic Data. However, these standards for data preservation and the ability to reproduce data must be updated, codified, and enforced before deregulation of certification is considered. Removing existing guardrails without a superior framework in place may actually facilitate fraudulent use of electronic health records.

Audit trails are foundational—not optional—to billing integrity, patient trust, and legal accountability. We must define what a "trustworthy record" is before we change the rules on how those records are certified. I ask that HHS pause the proposed certification changes until a modern, robust audit and preservation standard is finalized.  

Thank you for your time and your commitment to the health and safety of the American public.  

Sincerely,

Nursine S. Jackson, MSN, RN

title: Comment from Jain, Kanav, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Re: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability — Deregulatory Actions — Preserve Evidentiary Capacity and Contestability

Department of Health and Human Services  
ASTP/ONC

I submit this comment on the proposed Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability deregulatory actions. The rule proposes amendments to 45 CFR Parts 170 and 171. The authority citations include 42 U.S.C. 300jj–11, 42 U.S.C. 300jj–14, and 5 U.S.C. 552 for Part 170, and 42 U.S.C. 300jj–52 and 5 U.S.C. 552 for Part 171.

Burden reduction can be appropriate. However, there is a predictable failure mode where “deregulatory” changes reduce not only compliance overhead but also the evidentiary capacity required for accountability: the ability to reconstruct what happened, why it happened, and how it can be repaired.

1) Documentation and traceability are not paperwork — they are accountability infrastructure
    In health IT, the cost of insufficient logging and traceability is shifted downstream:
    • clinicians become private auditors,
    • patients become private archivists,
    • disputes become unwinnable because the record is incomplete.
    This is not efficiency; it is burden shifting.

2) Deregulation should not produce “read-only” accountability
    Interoperability and access rights depend on the ability to contest failures. Visibility without correction pathways produces procedural theater.

3) Recommended guardrails while streamlining compliance
    ASTP/ONC should ensure any deregulatory changes preserve:
    • Durable audit trails for behaviors that affect clinical decision-making and information access.
    • Attribution and traceability (who/what changed what, when, and under which configuration/policy).
    • Contestability pathways for disputes, especially those implicating information access and information-blocking claims.
    • Clocked response obligations when harm or access failures are reported, including escalation when deadlines are missed.

Closing:
Streamlining should reduce needless work without collapsing the record needed for remedy. If the system cannot reliably produce evidence, affected parties cannot contest outcomes, and accountability becomes a luxury good.

Respectfully submitted,
Kanav Jain
Independent researcher and systems designer (Chicago, IL)

title: Comment from Powell, Latricia, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
I support disciplined, targeted deregulation that reduces administrative burden and accelerates responsible innovation in health data interoperability. However, deregulatory actions must remain consistent with the Administrative Procedure Act, the 21st Century Cures Act, and HIPAA’s privacy and security framework. Deregulation should strengthen—not weaken—competition, patient trust, equity, and accountability, particularly as health data increasingly fuels AI-driven technologies and market consolidation pressures intensify.

⸻

To Whom It May Concern,

I submit this public comment in response to the Department of Health and Human Services’ proposed rule entitled Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions to Unleash Prosperity.

I comment from the perspective of an AI consultant, nonprofit executive, author, and public-interest advocate with formal training in psychology, business administration, and applied technology. My work focuses on the operational and legal consequences of health data policy across public institutions, technology developers, and community-based systems—particularly where innovation, equity, and governance intersect.

Regulatory and Market Context

Under the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), agency action must be reasoned, non-arbitrary, and supported by evidence. Under the 21st Century Cures Act, Congress made clear that interoperability and information access are core federal priorities, not optional market features. Any deregulatory action by ASTP/ONC should therefore preserve enforceable standards that prevent information blocking, protect patient agency, and promote fair competition.

Deregulation that weakens clarity or oversight risks substituting regulatory friction with market failure—particularly in a sector already prone to vendor lock-in, asymmetrical bargaining power, and opaque data practices.

Key Observations and Recommendations
1.Interoperability Must Remain a Public Good Under the Cures Act
While streamlining certification and compliance requirements may reduce costs for developers, interoperability standards must remain enforceable and technology-neutral, consistent with Congressional intent under the 21st Century Cures Act. Reduced oversight without substitute safeguards risks entrenching dominant EHR and data platform vendors, raising barriers to entry for startups, safety-net providers, and nonprofit innovators.
2.Equity Impacts Require Statutory Awareness and Measurable Safeguards
Deregulatory actions have foreseeable disparate impacts on populations already facing structural barriers to care and digital access. Consistent with civil rights obligations and federal equity directives, HHS should pair deregulation with explicit equity impact assessments, defined performance metrics, and post-implementation monitoring. Absent such measures, deregulation risks shifting costs and compliance burdens onto providers and communities least equipped to absorb them.
3.AI-Driven Health Innovation Requires Accountability to Sustain Market Confidence
Health data is now a core input for artificial intelligence, predictive analytics, and automated decision systems. Reducing transparency, auditability, or data provenance requirements in this context introduces systemic risk. Market growth depends on trust. Clear governance expectations around AI-enabled health technologies—particularly in clinical decision support, utilization management, and population health—are essential to preventing bias, preserving competition, and avoiding downstream legal and reputational harm.
4.Patient Agency and Consent Remain Central to a Functional Data Market
Efficient data exchange should not dilute patient rights established under HIPAA and reinforced through interoperability policy. Plain-language disclosures, meaningful consent, and enforceable remedies are not regulatory friction; they are trust infrastructure. Markets scale when participants—especially patients—understand and trust the rules of engagement.
5.Regulatory Certainty Is a Competitive Advantage
Predictable, clearly scoped rules lower transaction costs and attract responsible innovation. Overly rapid or ambiguous deregulation increases legal uncertainty, favors large incumbents with compliance leverage, and discourages smaller entrants who lack regulatory risk tolerance.

Conclusion

Prosperity in the health data ecosystem is not measured solely by reduced compliance lines or accelerated product cycles. It is measured by durable innovation, fair competition, patient trust, and equitable access. I urge HHS and ASTP/ONC to proceed with precision, retain core statutory protections, and commit to rigorous evaluation of market and equity impacts as these deregulatory actions are implemented.

title: Comment from Damick, David, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
ASTP committee::
With apology for not providing input earlier, I urge you to Please consider - and reconsider- the comments asking for stronger regulation which will bring clarity and transparency, rather then confusion and mistrust. There are already too many verified records of falsified records due to both human intervention and the weakly regulated integration of many LLM or "AI" programs.  Ensuring the integrity and security of electronic health information is vital. The proposed rule will do nothing to improve innovation in our hospitals, but will end the ability both internally and externally to check on what is real and what just sounds real.
I repeat what we have known from the beginning of computer-centered data handling (and as other professionals have noted) that the integrity of a medical record is only as reliable as its audit trail.  We must keep audit trail standards current with the use and progress of electronic medical records. Gone are the days when a provider wrote of a serious concern with a pen, and the urgency or tone could be interpreted by how large they wrote or the pressure on the pen, or perhaps an underline or punctuation.  In those days, if a record was changed there existed a strong rule for all of us that one just crossed out the deleted portion, and initialed or signed it then and there.  This CANNOT BE DISCARDED just because we now use computer inputs, and the security of the electronic medical record is the ONLY was this vital flow of information can work.  That is the integrity of the audit trail.  That is the reason that the audit trail standards were included with initial regulation,  and that is the only way a physician or patient can tell what went on - or what failed to go on- in their care.  Clearly, the current standard ASTM E2147-18, is outdated and insufficient and has seen so many interpretations is has lost its uniformity.  I concur with those urging HHS to urgently put forward a standard that addresses directly the many new PAC or medical device inputs and uses, and clearly and explicitly regulates the recording of ANY kind of "AI" or computer intelligence analysis used. Also, ASTM standards should specify that records systems record and disclose what software programs are used in patient care or analysis of patient information. And the principles of the original intent of HIPAA- that is interoperability and portability MUST be updated and made secure.  We already have EMR players attempting to "own" data and place their own contracts and profits over the purpose of electronic records for patient care and continuity. These standards are needed to address intentional fraud as well, as the security of even closed systems has been broken, continually and famously, many times.  Audit trails must be transparent and comprehensive, to show who, what and when entries and adjusrments are made. And because we encourage patient trust, these must be more clearly available to patients or their families or representatives.  That means we need standards to regulate (or even restrict) care-giver messaging about patients that cannot be stored or reproduced, like many of the vanishing cell phone and messaging programs used today. We are all glad to participate in the formation of proper and real standards that protect the record, yet do not inhibit innovation.  Such regulations would, i strongly believe, establish central policies that new innovations must attend to before being implemented in the records systems.  Not unlike safety rules for new vehicles- a manufacturer can make any vehicle they want, but to be on the public roads it needs headlights and brake lights and even brakes.  Not too much to ask out of standards.

title: Comment from Domnick, Sean, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
The integrity and security of electronic health information matters to me both personally and professionally. As a patient, I rely on medical records to be accurate, complete, and trustworthy. Clinical decisions are only as sound as the information on which they are based. If electronic health data can be altered or obscured without transparency, patient safety is put at risk.

Professionally, I see firsthand the consequences when electronic health information lacks integrity. Inaccurate or incomplete records undermine patient care, frustrate investigations, impair accountability, and erode public trust. Electronic medical records are no longer static charts. They are dynamic systems that drive clinical decisions, billing, quality reporting, and legal accountability.

Before any deregulation of certification is considered, standards for data creation, collection, preservation, and reproducibility must be updated. Electronic health data must be capable of being reliably reproduced in a usable, searchable, and shareable format. Without enforceable standards, deregulation risks weakening interoperability, transparency, and patient protection.

An updated audit trail standard is essential. Audit trails are critical to the security of electronic health information, to verifying data integrity, and to curtailing billing fraud. The current audit trail framework does not adequately reflect how modern electronic medical records function in real clinical environments.

Medical records have changed significantly since the last audit trail standard was published in 2018 (ASTM E2147-18). Today’s records incorporate copy-forward functionality, automated documentation, decision support tools, device-generated data, messaging platforms, and artificial intelligence. Standards must keep pace with these changes.

Any updated standard must address transparency in data creation and use by artificial intelligence, medical devices, and messaging systems. When data is generated or modified by software or AI, that fact must be visible and traceable.

Despite statutory requirements for access and transparency, patients still do not have meaningful access to their complete electronic health information. Records are often incomplete or stripped of metadata necessary to understand how the information was created or changed.

Software used in patient care and in the creation of the electronic medical record must be clearly identified in audit trails. Clinicians, patients, regulators, and investigators need to know not just what the record says, but how it was produced and by what systems.

Interoperability is essential for patient safety and lawful information sharing. Software must function safely within healthcare facilities and support compliant sharing of patient health information across systems, including compliance with the Trusted Exchange Framework and Common Agreement (TEFCA).

Data retention standards must be clearly defined. Electronic health data must be preserved for as long as patient care is ongoing, investigations are underway, or litigation holds are in place, and in compliance with applicable laws and regulations.

Audit trails are foundational—not optional—to billing integrity, patient trust, and legal accountability. Audit trails, including those applicable to artificial intelligence, must be transparent and non-proprietary. They must reveal corrections, deletions, copy-forwards, and overwrites. Without this visibility, electronic medical records can enable or conceal billing fraud.

Deregulation will not solve these problems. It risks facilitating practices that already allow hundreds of billions of dollars to be lost through the improper use of electronic medical records. Strong, modern, and enforceable standards are necessary to protect patients, safeguard public funds, and maintain trust in the healthcare system.

title: Comment from Vorhees, Erin, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Dear ASTP Representatives:

I write to you as both a health care consumer and a lawyer who has dedicated my career to advocating for safe patient care. Ensuring the integrity and security of electronic health information is vital to me professionally to ensure patient safety as well as transparency regarding the actions that occur during care and treatment and personally as a consumer of the American healthcare system. 

While I support the goal of enhancing interoperability, I am deeply concerned that the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability (HTI-5) proposed rule risks undermining these values by modifying certification requirements before modernizing foundational data standards. 
 
The Critical Need for Updated Audit Trail Standards:

The integrity of a medical record is only as reliable as its audit trail. An updated audit trail standard is required for the security of EHI, to verify its integrity, and to curtail billing fraud. The current standard, ASTM E2147-18, is outdated and insufficient for the modern clinical environment. Since 2018, the use and content of medical records have evolved dramatically. If we move forward with decertification or the removal of functional guardrails without first defining how data is created, collected, and preserved in a usable, searchable, and sharable format, we invite patient harm and compromise the trustworthiness of our healthcare system.  

To ensure medical records remain a "source of truth," HHS must prioritize a revision of the audit trail standard that includes:

AI, Medical Device, and Messaging Integration: 
Explicitly capturing the activities of AI algorithms, connected medical devices, and professional messaging to ensure clinical recommendations and data creation are transparent and traceable.

Software Identification and Interoperability: 
Audit trails must identify the specific software used in patient care. Furthermore, software must be interoperable for safe functionality within facilities and for sharing information in compliance with TEFCA.

Granular Metadata and Fraud Prevention: 
Standards must mandate the collection of precise data elements, including user roles and detailed activity logs (e.g., "copy-paste," "copy forwards," and "view-only" access). Audit trails that are transparent and reveal corrections, deletions, or overwrites, including the specific data that was changed, are essential to prevent the fraudulent practices that cost billions of dollars annually.

Patient Access: 
We must address the reality that many patients still do not have access to their EHI, despite such transparency being required by law. I frequently see clients that are unable to access complete medical records through patient portals and are repeatedly denied access to electronic medical records through requests directed to the provider. Further, when electronic records finally are provided in response to a request to the provider, they are frequently incomplete and providers and/or their third party vendors overcharge patients for access to their own electronic health information. 

Preservation Over Ephemerality
The move toward interoperability requires that data be not only shareable but also durable. Data retention must be clearly defined so that information is available as long as patient care, investigations, or litigation holds are ongoing, and to remain in compliance with state and federal regulations. I strongly urge HHS to prohibit "ephemeral messaging" or any automatic destruction of data. Data must be retained in a readable, reproducible format for as long as it remains in use. Allowing "ephemeral messaging" and/or not requiring messages sent between a patient's healthcare providers, regarding their care and treatment to be maintained as part of the patient's EHI, prevents patients and subsequent outside providers from knowing critical information regarding the decisions underlying their care and treatment. 

Sequential Implementation:
 I agree that we must establish clear, shared definitions for terms like ePHI, EHI, Generative AI, and Synthetic Data. However, these standards for data preservation and the ability to reproduce data must be updated, codified, and enforced before deregulation of certification is considered. Removing existing guardrails without a superior framework in place may actually facilitate fraudulent use of electronic health records.

Audit trails are foundational—not optional—to billing integrity, patient trust, and legal accountability. We must define what a "trustworthy record" is before we change the rules on how those records are certified. I ask that HHS pause the proposed certification changes until a modern, robust audit and preservation standard is finalized.  

Thank you for your time and your commitment to the health and safety of the American public. 
 
Sincerely,
Erin Vorhees​

title: Comment from Marshall, Jennifer, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
As a lawyer who has dedicated my career to advocating for patients injured by medical error, the integrity and security of electronic health information is critically important to my clients and personally as a consumer of the American healthcare system. Enhancing interoperability is a legitimate goal, however, it cannot be more important than a foundational assurance that the record itself has integrity and is secure from tampering and unauthorized access -- something I have seen numerous examples of in my cases. I and my colleagues remain deeply concerned that the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability (HTI-5) proposed rules are inadequate and undermine these values by modifying certification requirements before modernizing foundational data standards.

I echo the sentiments of colleagues who have commented here that the integrity of a medical record is only as reliable as its audit trail. Having seen many examples of record tampering, unauthorized access, and billing fraud in my legal practice, I agree that an updated audit trail standard is required for the security of EHI, to verify its integrity, and to curtail billing fraud. The current standard, ASTM E2147-18, is outdated and insufficient for the modern clinical environment. Since 2018, the use and content of medical records have evolved dramatically. If we move forward with decertification or the removal of functional guardrails without first defining how data is created, collected, and preserved in a usable, searchable, and sharable format, we invite patient harm and compromise the trustworthiness of our healthcare system.

I concur that certain elements must be prioritized in a revised and updated audit standard, to ensure that medical records can be relied upon for safe patient care, accurate billing, and as a legal record that stands as an objective source of truth suitable for patients, healthcare practitioners, CMS, and courts to rely upon. This includes:

1. Explicitly capturing the activities of AI algorithms, connected medical devices, and professional messaging to ensure clinical recommendations and data creation are transparent and traceable.

2. Audit trails must identify the specific software used in patient care. Furthermore, software must be interoperable for safe functionality within facilities and for sharing information in compliance with TEFCA.

3. Standards must mandate the collection of precise data elements, including user roles and detailed activity logs (e.g., "copy-paste," "copy forwards," and "view-only" access). Audit trails that are transparent and reveal corrections, deletions, or overwrites are essential to prevent the fraudulent practices that cost billions of dollars annually.

4. We must address the reality that many patients still do not have access to their EHI, despite such transparency being required by law.

5. Data retention policies must be clearly defined so that information is available as long as patient care, investigations, or litigation holds are ongoing, and to remain in compliance with state and federal regulations. 

6. I strongly urge HHS to prohibit "ephemeral messaging" or any automatic destruction of data. Data must be retained in a readable, reproducible format for as long as it remains in use.

7. Clear, shared definitions for terms like ePHI, EHI, Generative AI, Synthetic Data and others are needed. However, these standards for data preservation and the ability to reproduce data must be updated, codified, and enforced before deregulation of certification is considered. Removing existing guardrails without a superior framework in place may actually facilitate fraudulent use of electronic health records.

Audit trails are foundational—not optional—to billing integrity, patient trust, and legal accountability. We must define what a "trustworthy record" is before we change the rules on how those records are certified. I join my colleagues in asking that HHS pause the proposed certification changes until a modern, robust audit and preservation standard is finalized.

Thank you for your time and your commitment to the health and safety of the American public.

title: Comment from Hamilton, Molly, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
Comment from Barnes, MD, JD, Stephen, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
ASTP representatives:

I write to you asking that the ASTP/ONC proposed rule on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability be paused until certain underlying audit trail standards, some of which have been in place since 2018 but are now being modernized, are finalized.  

I am a board certified surgeon as well as licensed healthcare attorney.  In both roles, I have dedicated my career to advocating for safe patient care. My 30+ year career in healthcare has seen the demise of traditional “paper” records and rise of electronic health information systems/electronic health record systems (EHS and EHR).  While EHS and EHR supposedly increase efficiency and portability of patient records, EHS and EHR pose a risk of allowing post-care entries in the medical record to masquerade as contemporaneously entered entries.  Therefore, ensuring the integrity, credibility, accuracy, and security of electronic health information is vital to me professionally to ensure patient safety and personally as a consumer of the American healthcare system. While I support the goal of enhancing interoperability, I am deeply concerned that the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability (HTI-5) proposed rule risks undermining these values by modifying certification requirements before modernizing foundational data standards.

Audit Trails Standards – The Critical Need for Updated Audit Trail Standards
The integrity, credibility, accuracy, and security of a medical record is only as reliable as its audit trail. An updated audit trail standard is required for the security of EHR, to verify its integrity, and to curtail medical record and medical billing fraud. The current standard, ASTM E2147-18, while robust for its timeframe of 2018, is now insufficient for the modern clinical environment. Since 2018, the use and content of medical records have evolved dramatically. If we move forward with decertification or the removal of functional guardrails without first defining how data is created, collected, and preserved in a usable, searchable, and sharable format, we invite patient harm and compromise the trustworthiness of patient medical records and thus our healthcare system.

To ensure medical records remain a transparent source of credible and truthful record entries, including the current ASTM standard who, what, where, and when such entries were made, but now requiring far more, HHS must prioritize a revision of the audit trail standard that includes:

AI, Medical Device, and Messaging Integration: Explicitly capturing the activities of AI algorithms, connected medical devices, and professional messaging to ensure clinical recommendations and data creation are transparent and traceable.

Software Identification and Interoperability: Audit trails must identify the specific software used in patient care. Furthermore, software must be interoperable for safe functionality within facilities and for sharing information in compliance with TEFCA.

Granular Metadata and Fraud Prevention: Standards must mandate the collection of precise data elements, including user roles and detailed activity logs (e.g., "copy-paste," "copy forwards," and "view-only" access). Audit trails that are transparent and reveal corrections, deletions, or overwrites are essential to prevent the fraudulent practices that cost billions of dollars annually.

Patient Access: We must address the reality that many patients still do not have access to their EHI, despite such transparency being required by law.

Preservation Over Ephemerality
The move toward interoperability requires that data be not only shareable but also durable. Data retention must be clearly defined so that information is available as long as patient care, investigations, or litigation holds are ongoing, and to remain in compliance with state and federal regulations. I strongly urge HHS to prohibit "ephemeral messaging" or any automatic destruction of data. Data must be retained in a readable, reproducible format for as long as it remains in use.

Sequential Implementation
I agree that we must establish clear, shared definitions for terms like ePHI, EHI, Generative AI, and Synthetic Data. However, these standards for data preservation and the ability to reproduce data must be updated, codified, and enforced before deregulation of certification is considered. Removing existing guardrails without a superior framework in place may actually facilitate fraudulent use of electronic health records.

Audit trails are fundamental to medical record and medical billing integrity, patient trust, and legal accountability. We must define what a “trustworthy” record is before we change the rules on how those records are certified. 

I request that HHS pause the proposed certification changes until a modern, robust audit and preservation standard is finalized.

Thank you for your time in reviewing my comment.

Sincerely,

Stephen A. Barnes, MD, JD, FACLM
Houston, Texas

title: Comment from Daue, Craig, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896

I am an attorney who has been practicing law in Montana for forty-nine years. During most of my career, I have represented hospitals, physicians, and patients in medical malpractice litigation. title: Comment from Ozak, Laura , HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text: I am a BSN registered nurse (critical care) and lawyer.  I "grew up" in health care, so to speak.  My entire adult career has been centered around being a voice for patients, including many elders. Over the past 40 years I have witnessed the dramatic changes as health care has evolved to become enterprise systems that fiercely compete for patients' health care dollars.  Lost in all of this is PATIENT AUTONOMY.  Patients have largely lost control over their personal health information (PHI), as every day we learn of ways in which PHI data is sold and/or used for purposes other than health care delivery.  This includes the "mining" of PHI by coders in large enterprises to enhance billing to Medicare and other insurers.  To that end, I attach the DOJ's complaint against one such health care enterprise, Kaiser Permanente.  The extent of this mining process is mind boggling.  I have also witnessed attempts at manipulating health care data when bad outcomes occur.  The only way to discover such manipulation is vis a vis audit trails (which can also be used to exonerate health care providers). Patients already face a laborious task to obtain and decipher audit trail information with the current standards in place.  Placing further road blocks will harm patients and violate their fundamental right to control how their PHI is used.  Removing existing guardrails without a superior process in place will be devastating.  It will encourage fraud.  HHS needs to pause and reflect on its true mission, and pause and reflect on how wholesale abrogation of current guardrails - without a superior replacement framework- will only serve to victimize and harm patients who depend upon the government for protection.  Yours truly, Laura Ozak, RN, JD.

title: Comment from J Tolin Law, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Before becoming a lawyer, I was a critical care RT for many years long before EMR existed. Watching an anesthesiologist tear up his record and rewrite after failing to recognize a case of malignant hyperthermia, caused the death of a 23 year old pro football player. 

Accurate records are essential for patients as well as any advocates for patients. Deregulation is not only a bad idea, but encourages software providers  to misuse, obscure, or make any audit trails ever harder to be accurate and complete . One of the biggest EMR providers is EPIC. They have already found a way to circumvent anything harmful in the EMR's by the use of secure chat. Secure chat is a method for healthcare providers to communicate with each other and are not considered part of the medical records. When a medical record affidavit comes in stating the records are complete and accurate, it is not true or accurate with EPIC and encourages both fraud and the ability of healthcare professional's  to avoid pointing fingers or being truthful as to what is happening on patients. An example of this occurred in a recent case where a surgeon refused to come in despite urging from the critical care NP, and when I asked for the secure chat communication in our legal discovery process, the discussions were enough to cause the case to get settled.

Deregulation would just encourage more behavior like this and is counter-productive to finding out the truth, whether it be a patient or an advocate. I would strongly oppose any deregulation as truth and transparency is what is needed, and the best way to get an accurate representation of what occurred. 

I would also encourage regulations that require all communication made in EMR's to be made part of the record, to avoid loopholes like Secure chat.

With regards, Josh Tolin

title: Comment from thomas, isobel, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
The integrity and security of electronic health information matters to me both personally and professionally. As a patient, I rely on medical records to be accurate, complete, and trustworthy. Clinical decisions are only as sound as the information on which they are based. If electronic health data can be altered or obscured without transparency, patient safety is put at risk.

Professionally, I see firsthand the consequences when electronic health information lacks integrity. Inaccurate or incomplete records undermine patient care, frustrate investigations, impair accountability, and erode public trust. Electronic medical records are no longer static charts. They are dynamic systems that drive clinical decisions, billing, quality reporting, and legal accountability.

Before any deregulation of certification is considered, standards for data creation, collection, preservation, and reproducibility must be updated. Electronic health data must be capable of being reliably reproduced in a usable, searchable, and shareable format. Without enforceable standards, deregulation risks weakening interoperability, transparency, and patient protection.

An updated audit trail standard is essential. Audit trails are critical to the security of electronic health information, to verifying data integrity, and to curtailing billing fraud. The current audit trail framework does not adequately reflect how modern electronic medical records function in real clinical environments.

Medical records have changed significantly since the last audit trail standard was published in 2018 (ASTM E2147-18). Today’s records incorporate copy-forward functionality, automated documentation, decision support tools, device-generated data, messaging platforms, and artificial intelligence. Standards must keep pace with these changes.

Any updated standard must address transparency in data creation and use by artificial intelligence, medical devices, and messaging systems. When data is generated or modified by software or AI, that fact must be visible and traceable.

Despite statutory requirements for access and transparency, patients still do not have meaningful access to their complete electronic health information. Records are often incomplete or stripped of metadata necessary to understand how the information was created or changed.

Software used in patient care and in the creation of the electronic medical record must be clearly identified in audit trails. Clinicians, patients, regulators, and investigators need to know not just what the record says, but how it was produced and by what systems.

Interoperability is essential for patient safety and lawful information sharing. Software must function safely within healthcare facilities and support compliant sharing of patient health information across systems, including compliance with the Trusted Exchange Framework and Common Agreement (TEFCA).

Data retention standards must be clearly defined. Electronic health data must be preserved for as long as patient care is ongoing, investigations are underway, or litigation holds are in place, and in compliance with applicable laws and regulations.

Audit trails are foundational—not optional—to billing integrity, patient trust, and legal accountability. Audit trails, including those applicable to artificial intelligence, must be transparent and non-proprietary. They must reveal corrections, deletions, copy-forwards, and overwrites. Without this visibility, electronic medical records can enable or conceal billing fraud.

Deregulation will not solve these problems. It risks facilitating practices that already allow hundreds of billions of dollars to be lost through the improper use of electronic medical records. Strong, modern, and enforceable standards are necessary to protect patients, safeguard public funds, and maintain trust in the healthcare system.

title: Comment from KELSO, RONDA, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Dear ASTP representatives:
As an RN who practiced back when we had handwritten notes, one of the most important things about documentation in medical records was instruction on how to correct a medical record. The instruction was simple and very easy to spot ... a single line through the inaccurate documentation with your initials next to the changed record. The reader could see what was originally entered and the new entry simultaneously. You could also see whether or not the entry was changed at the time the entry was made or if the change was made at some later date. The instructions for that type of an entry were to write "late entry for (date and time) entered on (date and time):" followed by the change. These entries were the old school audit trail. This kept the author honest and the integrity of the record preserved. it is imperative that we continue to have medical records which when altered, deleted or addended are tracked. 
As an attorney, it is extremely important to understand when an entry was made, by whom and what was said. Additionally audit trails are used to determine who views records and if not authorized to view records, can be held for privacy violations.  
Please consider these comments whne making decisions about audit trails and the purpose they serve for the safety of the patient and integrity of the record.

title: Comment from Snyder, Brian, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
I submit my comment to this proposal as an attorney who frequently deals with audit trails. As those involved are aware, the purpose of the audit trail is to ensure integrity of the record. We cannot have a system where the patient or the provider cannot be sure the records are correct and accurate. With the current system, the audit trail tracks who accessed records, when he/she accessed them, and what was done to the record. If those safeguard are taken away, we will be left unaware if the records can be trusted. This is just as important to the healthcare provider as it is to the patient. For the provider, if he or she is sued without good reason, the provider should be able to rely on the records to prove his/her defense. For the patient, it is crucial to know if the records are reliable or if they have been manipulated. 

The other important element to this is that audit trails are critical in the fight to prevent healthcare fraud. Without knowing if records were tampered with, there will be no ability to determine if the charges/treatment were appropriate. 

We need systems in place to keep people honest. Without regulated audit trails, healthcare will become the wild west. This cannot happen, as it endangers patients and will exponentially increase healthcare fraud, which affects every taxpayer in our country. 

Brian Snyder
Attorney

title: Comment from Hunt, Taylor, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Dear ASTP Representatives,

I am a health care consumer and consultant who has spent extensive time working to ensure the integrity and security of electronic health information. A cornerstone of this work is the use of audit trails as a mechanism for establishing a reliable source of truth in medical records. These safeguards are critical to patient safety, accountability, and trust in the healthcare system.

While I support the goal of enhancing interoperability, I am deeply concerned that the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability (HTI-5) proposed rule risks undermining these foundational values by modifying certification requirements before modernizing and stabilizing the underlying data standards.

An updated audit trail standard is essential to protect the security of electronic health information, verify data integrity, and curtail billing fraud. Audit trails serve as a primary control for detecting inappropriate access, unauthorized modification, and inaccurate attribution of clinical and financial data. Weakening or deferring these standards introduces risk at a time when healthcare data volume, velocity, and complexity continue to increase.

Additionally, standards must keep pace with the evolving use and content of medical records. Clinical documentation, data exchange, and secondary data use have changed substantially since the last audit trail standard, ASTM E2147-18, was published in 2018. The current regulatory approach does not adequately reflect the realities of modern electronic health records, longitudinal data aggregation, and cross-organizational data exchange.

Any updated audit trail standard must also explicitly address transparency in data creation and use by artificial intelligence, medical devices, and electronic messaging systems. These technologies increasingly contribute to clinical decision-making and documentation, yet their involvement is often opaque within the record. Without clear auditability, it becomes difficult to determine authorship, provenance, and accountability for data generated or modified by automated systems.

Finally, despite legal requirements for access and transparency, patients still do not have meaningful or comprehensive access to their electronic health information. Strengthening audit trail standards should support—not hinder—patients’ ability to understand who has accessed their data, how it has been used, and how clinical information about them is created and maintained.

For these reasons, I urge ASTP to prioritize the modernization of audit trail and data integrity standards as a prerequisite to changes in certification requirements under HTI-5, ensuring that interoperability advances do not come at the expense of patient safety, transparency, and trust.

title: Comment from Middlesex Health, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
February 4, 2026

Re: Comments to “Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity” 

Middlesex Health wishes to express our support for the proposed revisions to the definitions of “access” and “use” of Electronic Health Information (EHI) under 45 CFR 171.102, which explicitly include automation technologies such as robotic process automation (RPA) and autonomous artificial intelligence systems (Agentic AI).

This regulatory clarity is crucial for the future of health information technology and patient care. The explicit codification that “access” and “use” apply to automated means will achieve several key objectives:

- Provide Essential Regulatory Clarity: By explicitly naming RPA and Agentic AI, the proposal removes ambiguity, allowing hospitals and developers to invest confidently in and deploy these transformative technologies to improve efficiency and enhance the delivery of care without fear of inadvertently violating information blocking regulations.

- Strengthen Anti-Information Blocking Efforts: The proposal reinforces that actors who interfere with the automated access, exchange, or use of EHI may be engaging in information blocking. This protection is vital to ensure that AI systems, which rely on seamless, automated access to EHI for their operational integrity, can function as intended to support clinical and administrative workflows.

- Promote Health System Efficiency: As the rule acknowledges, the increased use of automated means is key to improving efficiency. Establishing a clear framework for automated data use will accelerate the adoption of AI and RPA solutions that can drive down costs and reduce administrative burden.

These proposed revisions are a necessary and timely update to ensure that the spirit of the Cures Act, which promotes the seamless access and use of health data, is upheld in the age of advanced automation and artificial intelligence.

Sincerely,

Adriel Kramer, MD
Chief Medical Information Officer
Middlesex Health

title: Comment from SLI Compliance, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
As an authorized certification body, we noticed some details in this proposed regulation that may benefit from clarification and/or streamlining of processes.

Real World Testing and SVAP:

Regarding the proposed requirement “(3) permitting the use of SVAP for the remaining non-API real world testing certification criteria with minimal reporting requirements” at https://www.federalregister.gov/d/2025-23896/p-413:

At https://www.federalregister.gov/d/2025-23896/p-418, the proposed rule indicates “…for health IT certified to the remaining real world testing certification criteria identified in § 170.405(a), developers will only need to identify in their real world testing results their voluntary updates (health IT products, certification criteria, and standards/implementation specifications) to standards and implementation specifications that the National Coordinator has approved through SVAP…” 

While we acknowledge the requirement included at 45 CFR 170.405(b)(2)(ii)(C), we question the extra burden of developers being required submit a RWT Results Report for non-API criteria solely to indicate SVAP information that is already available on the product’s CHPL listing. Developers are already required to notify their ACB and their customers prior to updating their product to use SVAP standards, and the SVAP standards and SVAP Notice URL are already included on the product’s listing. We question the value of requiring developers to submit a Real World Test Results Report containing only that same information.

If it is finalized that a developer is required to submit a RWT Results Report for any SVAP standards implemented for non-API criteria, please clarify what SVAP standards need to be reported (e.g., must a developer submit a RWT Report for any SVAP standards on the product’s CHPL listing at the time of reporting, OR must a developer submit a RWT Report only for any SVAP standards implemented during the calendar year to which the real world testing applies to, OR must a developer submit a RWT Report only for any SVAP standards listed on CHPL at the time of reporting that have not previously been included in a RWT Results Report, etc.). 

Our suggestion is to require inclusion of the SVAP information on the RWT Results Report for only the g10 and g31-33 criteria since the developer will be required to submit a RWT Results Report for those criteria. We suggest not requiring any RWT Results Reports to be submitted for any non-API criteria regardless of whether or not SVAP standards were used since the same SVAP information is already included on CHPL. This will help reduce confusion and burden for developers.

170.315(f)(5):

At https://www.federalregister.gov/d/2025-23896/page-61032 and https://www.federalregister.gov/d/2025-23896/p-310, it sounds like the proposal is to revert back to requiring paragraph (f)(5)(i). We have concerns with this proposal because all developers are currently required to comply with paragraph (f)(5)(ii) by February 28, 2026, to meet the HTI-1 requirements (without meeting the standards, per the Enforcement Discretion Notice). After just attesting to meeting paragraph (f)(5)(ii), with or without the standards, in order to meet HTI-1 requirements, developers would then be required to revert back to meeting the requirements of (f)(5)(i). This would require another developer attestation to attest that they still meet the (f)(5)(i) standards. For example, paragraph (f)(5)(i) specifically refers to standards at 170.213, 170.207(i), and 170.207(a)(1), which are not included in paragraph (f)(5)(ii). We believe reverting back to (f)(5)(i) would create additional burden for developers in requiring them to ensure their product meets those requirements and attesting to paragraph (f)(5)(i) again. Our suggestion is to require developers to meet the requirements of (f)(5)(ii) with the standards removed, as the Enforcement Discretion currently requires.

Incorrect 170.315(e)(1) References:

The references for e1 ambulatory setting and inpatient appear to be incorrect. At https://www.federalregister.gov/d/2025-23896/p-817, 170.315(e)(1)(B)(2)(i) for ambulatory setting should reference (e)(1)(i)(A)(2), (3), (4), (6), and (7). Also, at https://www.federalregister.gov/d/2025-23896/p-818, 170.315(e)(1)(B)(2)(ii) for inpatient setting should reference (e)(1)(i)(A)(2), (3), (5), (6), and (7).

title: Comment from Herman, Dustin , HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Imagine a patient is sent home from an ER, but hourse later, blood tests come back showing the patient has a blood infection. But no one calls the patient and the patient dies. The doctor says he sent an electronic message to a nurse instructing the nurse to call the patient. The nurse says she never got the message. Later when the patient's family wants to see the messages, the hospital says those messages are automatically deleted after 7 days, so nobody can see what messages were or were not sent about the patient's care. This story is based on a real case. And messages like this about patient care are being automatically deleted every day in this country. 

We need better audit trail standards to ensure transparency and patient access to medical records. Before any deregulation of certification is considered, standards for data creation, collection, preservation, and reproducibility must be updated.

I am a healthcare consumer along with my wife and kids, as well as an attorney who represents patients in medical malpractice lawsuits. There is a critical need to update the audit trail standards to ensure transparency in healthcare. Patients must be able to trust their medical records--and must have access to all their medical records. Providers should never be permitted to communicate about patient care in a messaging app, but avoid having those messages saved in the EMR. Patients have the right to read any messages between providers regarding their care. Right now, if you walk into any hospital in the country, you will see nurses and doctors essentially sending text messages back and forth to each other about patients. This is great, and allows healthcare to be delivered more efficiently, but the problem is those messages are often not saved. And that is by design by the hosptial systems. Many of the messages are automatically deleted in 7 days, even though the messages were critical to the providers delivering care to a patient. The audit trail standards should be updated to include audit data for any message that is sent regarding patient care. Furthermore, AI is rapidly evolving, and patients have a right to know if AI suggestions or algorithms were used or followed by their doctors. Audit trails are foundational—not optional—to billing integrity, patient trust, and legal accountability. We must define what a "trustworthy record" is before we change the rules on how those records are certified. 

I ask that HHS pause the proposed certification changes until a modern, robust audit and preservation standard is finalized.

title: Comment on HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
While the intent is to decrease burden, removing some of the requirements will have impacts downstream. It allows the EMR vendors to not incorporate key functionality into the base CEHRT edition that are necessary for regulatory reporting and other initiatives. For example, removing auditable events, audit reports and amendments will lead to lack of information needed for breach and other types of investigations. Vendor may opt to no add tracking functionality on new tables/fields, etc. Health centers frequently use auditable events and reports to track changes in a patient's medical record for complaints and/or privacy concerns. Likewise, as patients become more aware of their digital record accounting of disclosures are being requested. With this being removed, vendors will not be required to ensure there is an ease of reporting. While decreasing the vendor burden, it is inflating providers and health care entity burden to track and produce outside of the CEHRT. 

Transition of Care (CCD-A) information continues to bring much frustration without requirements of codified data that must be included. Health care providers often receive a document that contain no codified or relevant information for continuity. It has been a check the box for Promoting Interoperability rather than the intent it was supposed to have. I recommend instead of removing to add language surrounding a minimum data set of codified information that must be included in the transmission for continuity of care. It would be ideal that any provider who received payments from Medicare/Medicaid must abide by the requirements. This will provide a strong foundation for reducing health care cost and improving patient outcomes.

It would also be helpful if CMS provided transparent and clear guidance on when a CEHRT has to be implemented in order to participate in programs that require use of a CEHRT. This is a grey area and personally received conflicting guidance from CMS inquires. 

In summary, this rule has several areas intended to reduce burden. However, at whose cost does this come. In many instances, allowing vendors to not be held to specific requirements for inclusion in the base edition CEHRT will open the door to increased burden of individual providers and health care providing entities.

title: Comment from McGruder-Jarman, Lois, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
De-regulation tends to be a slippery slope and considering the frequency of data breaches that continue even at this late date I don't think that not putting certain block measures in place is a smart move.  This is about protecting people's privacy and as someone who has unfortunately experienced multiple medical breaches I feel very strongly about this both as a patient and as employee of multiple medical facilities. And like people in real life, AI can be influenced by bad data as evidenced by chatbots on various platforms: racist rhetoric, hate speech and degradation of women are just a few offending topics that were featured prominently.

In short, the system needs to implement more stringent measures to protect information not loosen the reins so to speak

Deregulation is the reduction or elimination of government rules, laws, and restrictions imposed on businesses and industries. It aims to promote competition, lower consumer prices, increase efficiency, and encourage innovation by reducing compliance burdens. While it often leads to lower prices and more choices, it can also cause market instability, monopolies, and reduced safety standards.

title: Comment from Belamaric, Owen, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text: Although I applaud the use of AI to synthesize new forms of proteins, which is a great medical advancement, I think the use of AI to provide psychological services is dangerous in the extreme. A psychologically-focused AI misused would greatly exacerbate public unrest and psychological issues, as well as damaging public trust that psychology is a useful tool to help people heal mental health issues. Additionally, the profit motive of a private company marketing AI software cannot be rationalized with a physician’s duty of care towards their patients. This is not a new piece of medical equipment, it is a robot that is designed to manipulate text to create coherent sentences. It does not reason or think in any way we imagine it, and cannot be adequately used to diagnose issues patients or doctors might seek its input on. It is a text generator which makes sense more often than not, not a thinking machine.

title: Comment from Fairview Health Services, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Dear Dr. Keane,

Fairview Health Services (Fairview) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy/Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology (ASTP/ONC) Deregulatory Actions to Unleash Prosperity (HTI-5) Proposed Rule. 

Fairview is a nonprofit, integrated health system providing exceptional health care across the full spectrum of medical services through our 10 hospitals and 60 clinics. Fairview’s broad care continuum includes academic and community hospitals, primary and specialty care clinics, senior and long-term care facilities, retail and specialty pharmacies, pharmacy benefit management services, rehabilitation centers, counseling, home health care services, and an integrated provider network. Fairview’s 34,000+ employees and 5,000+ system providers embrace innovation and new thinking to drive a healthier future through healing, discovery, and education.

Fairview values ONC’s focus on reducing administrative burden, strengthening interoperability, and modernizing the regulatory foundation to support Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) based Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) and AI enabled data exchange. We offer the following feedback grounded in our operational experience, technology ecosystem, and previous comments on ONC rulemaking. Fairview appreciates ONC’s continued efforts to reduce administrative burden by building on its action to address multiagency reporting burden, improving coordination across Health Information Exchanges (HIEs) and Qualified Health Information Network (QHIN) ecosystems, and eliminating workflow burdens needed to achieve better treatment-oriented data transfer between health systems. We request that ONC designate resources to help organizations become part of a QHIN, provide appropriate QHIN oversite, require QHIN’s to share data effectively in a standardized way, and require EHR vendors to take that data and show it to physicians and care givers in a way that is consistent with their workflow. This will greatly enhance the ability for patients to navigate health systems to get optimal care with efficiency for providers. ONC should also structure the Trusted Exchange Framework and Common Agreement (TEFCA) network in a way that QHIN’s can help with federal and state public reporting for regulatory purposes and share standardized quality data as the next goals for interoperability through TEFCA. The most dangerous elements from this proposed deregulation are:
•Loss of AI transparency artifacts (model cards) leaving a large burden on health systems with significant inefficiency and risk to determine safety and quality of AI tools
•High risk longitudinal data like family history, problem lists, and social data not having appropriate standards for dates and clinical context causing inaccurate AI interpretations and clinical errors due to poor quality data and potentially issues with patient care if the information is not standard functionality
•Erosion of certified clinical decision support (CDS) to place most of the burden on provider organizations and not EHR-vendors. Also, there is a need to ensure that providers and other clinicians stay in the loop for designing what things can be autonomous and what things need clinical oversight with AI guidance instead of being fully autonomous 
•Transfer of AI safety accountability to providers and health systems without continued regulatory oversite of vendor AI development through FDA and other regulatory mechanisms
•Potential abuse of individual patient data by opening access to non-provider organization entities without HIPAA regulation and decreasing oversight
•Erosion of cybersecurity standards including removal of encryption standards for authentication credentials and other standards which will put health systems at risk for cybersecurity attacks and data leakage  

Thank you for consideration of Fairview’s comments on the HTI-5 proposed rule. Fairview values ONC’s focus on reducing administrative burden, strengthening interoperability, and modernizing the regulatory foundation to support FHIR-based APIs and AI-enabled data exchange. 

Please see attached for Fairview Health Services' full comments.

title: Comment from Matyac, Jennifer, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
I strongly oppose the HTI-5 proposed rule’s approach of weakening certification requirements before modernizing the foundational data standards that ensure the safety, integrity, and reliability of electronic health information. The integrity of a medical record is only as reliable as its audit trail.  Before deregulation of certification is considered, standards for data integrity must be strengthened and modernized including updating the audit trail standard.  The current standard, ASTM E2147-18, is outdated and insufficient for the modern clinical environment.  Electronic health records must be capable of being reliably reproduced in a usable, searchable, and interoperable format, with preserved context, metadata, and provenance.  

The integrity, reliability, and auditability of electronic health records is foundational to patient safety, quality of care, interoperability, and accountability across the healthcare system. The accuracy, completeness, and functional integrity of records used in patient care, clinical decision-making, billing, compliance, and litigation must be prioritized.  I am deeply concerned that HTI-5 risks undermining these core principles by relaxing certification requirements before modernizing the foundational data standards on which safe and reliable health information exchange depends. 

Health information technology standards and certification requirements exist to ensure interoperability, safeguard patient access to data, and prevent fragmentation across systems. Eliminating or weakening these requirements does not reduce burden—it removes safeguards in a domain where errors jeopardize patient safety and carry irreversible consequences.

For these reasons, I urge HHS to reconsider deregulatory actions that remove or dilute certification requirements.  Any modernization of standards should strengthen—not diminish—assurances of safety, integrity, transparency, and accountability in electronic health information systems.

Thank you.

Sincerely,

Jennifer Matyac, RN, BSN, JD

title: Comment from Orion Health (a HEALWELL Company), HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
On behalf of Orion Heath, I am responding to the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy (ASTP)/Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology (ONC) (collectively, ASTP/ONC), Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) Heath Data, Technology, and Interoperability (HTI-5): ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions to Unleash Prosperity proposed rule published in the December 29, 2025 Federal Register.  

Orion Health, a HEALWELL AI company, is a global healthcare technology firm specializing in interoperability, health data infrastructure, and population-level data integration. In the United States, Orion Health partners with state governments, health systems, health information exchanges, clinically integrated networks, and risk-bearing entities to enable secure data exchange, improve care coordination, and support value-based care initiatives. 

With more than 30 years of experience supporting large-scale health data platforms globally, Orion Health brings an infrastructure-first perspective to operationalizing health IT standards at scale. Our solutions are designed to connect disparate data sources, support governance and data quality, and help organizations translate data access into operational readiness across clinical, administrative, and public health use cases. 

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 

Orion Health supports the objectives of HTI-5 to reduce unnecessary administrative burden across the healthcare system. We agree that simplification is needed; however, it must not come at the expense of interoperability as a core, enforceable requirement. Interoperability must remain a non-negotiable foundation of the health IT ecosystem. 

Drawing on our experience supporting health data exchange across regional and national communities, we believe burden reduction efforts should focus on eliminating duplicative documentation and manual workflows, rather than weakening the technical standards that enable meaningful interoperability in practice. FHIR-based APIs should remain the default mechanism for health information exchange, supporting timely, secure, and scalable data sharing across care delivery, public health, and community-based partners. Interoperability should be measurable in performance, reliability, and real-world usability, not declared through API availability. 

As certification requirements are streamlined, corresponding expectations for vendor accountability must remain clear. Health IT developers should be required to demonstrate real, testable interoperability in production environments, maintain strong privacy and security protections, avoid information blocking, and ensure that information sharing remains the default unless a legitimate legal, safety, or privacy concern applies. Moving beyond read-only exchange toward automation-ready interoperability will be essential to achieving the efficiency gains envisioned by this rule.  

Where HTI-5 introduces or enables AI-supported capabilities, transparency is essential. Vendors should clearly communicate the purpose, intended use, and limitations of AI systems, particularly when such technologies are used to support clinical efficiency or decision-making. Clear disclosure is necessary to support trust, appropriate use, and regulatory oversight. Responsible AI deployment depends on complete, timely, and standardized data flows; interoperability is therefore a prerequisite to safe and scalable AI adoption. 

HTI-5 represents an important opportunity to reduce costs while improving the usability, reliability, and trustworthiness of health data. Orion Health supports reforms that accelerate innovation while preserving interoperability as a foundational requirement, and we appreciate the opportunity to provide feedback as ASTP/ONC advances this framework.
