title: Comment from Joshi, Satyadhar, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Re: Comment on Proposed Rule — Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions to Unleash Prosperity
Docket ID: HHS-ONC-2025-0005
Dear Office of the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy / Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology,
I am submitting this comment as an independent researcher in response to the above-referenced proposed rule published December 29, 2025. My comment is accompanied by an attached academic working paper:
Interoperability Agentic Gen AI for API-Driven Health IT: Advancing the Modernized US ONC Framework
Satyadhar Joshi (February 2026)
This paper analyzes the proposed rule's deregulatory provisions from the perspective of agentic generative AI systems, FHIR-based API interoperability, and AI governance. It draws on over 100 academic, industry, and policy sources to argue that while the rule's FHIR-forward strategy creates essential technical conditions for autonomous AI in healthcare, certain deregulatory actions — particularly the removal of AI transparency mandates and privacy/security certification criteria — create governance gaps that warrant complementary policy measures.

title: Comment from InterSystems Corporation, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)
Office of the Secretary
Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy (ASTP) / Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology (ONC)
Proposed Rule Comments
Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity (HTI-5)
45 CFR Parts 170 and 171
RIN 0955–AA09
Submitted via: Regulations.gov
Comment deadline: February 27, 2026 (no later than 5:00 p.m. ET)
Prepared by: InterSystems
Points of Contact: Zach Keating | pr@intersystems.com

Preamble, Section I.A.2.b “America’s FHIR-Forward Future”
InterSystems supports ASTP/ONC’s direction to prioritize HL7 FHIR-based exchange as the foundation for scalable, automated interoperability. We share ASTP/ONC’s objective to modernize exchange beyond document-centric paradigms, and we agree that FHIR’s resource-based model enables more granular, discrete data exchange than document-centric approaches such as C-CDA, supporting workflow automation, decision support, analytics, and longitudinal record assembly without forcing implementers into the ambiguity of document parsing.
InterSystems encourages ASTP/ONC to emphasize that a “FHIR-forward” approach should advance computable interoperability - where exchanged data is reliably structured, attributable, and usable - not simply accessible. We believe this will reduce integration burden, improve data quality, and accelerate innovation across care delivery, payer, public health, and third-party application ecosystems.
Preamble, Section I.A.2.b “America’s FHIR-Forward Future” AI-enabled interoperability (FHIR + MCP): trust, security, and governance
InterSystems appreciates ASTP/ONC’s recognition that AI-enabled interoperability may emerge through approaches that combine FHIR-based exchange with newer standards such as Model Context Protocol (MCP). 
InterSystems recommends that ASTP/ONC frame MCP-like approaches as adjacent to, not a substitute for, core interoperability standards (FHIR/USCDI). AI context-sharing mechanisms should not bypass established requirements for standardized data representation, secure access controls, and operational accountability.

To support safe, scalable AI-enabled interoperability, InterSystems recommends guardrails such as: (a) strong authentication/authorization and least-privilege access; (b) provenance and traceability for source data and AI outputs; (c) auditability/logging appropriate to clinical risk; and (d) governance expectations that discourage proprietary ‘AI-only’ side channels that fragment exchange.

Part 170 discussion, Section III.A.2.a Care Coordination Certification Criteria, “Transitions of Care”
InterSystems supports ASTP/ONC’s continued movement toward FHIR-based, discrete interoperability. However, we recommend ASTP/ONC reconsider the proposed revisions to 170.315(b)(1) that would remove the ability to “create” and to “send” transitions-of-care C-CDA documents and narrow certification primarily to “receive and validate.” 
Transitions of care remain a core, high-volume exchange workflow across heterogeneous settings, and document-based exchange will continue in the near term - particularly across cross-vendor transitions and long-term/post-acute care. If certification no longer covers create/send capabilities, implementation consistency may degrade and shift integration burden downstream to providers, HIE/HIN operators, and recipients.
InterSystems recommends ASTP/ONC maintain a baseline, end-to-end transitions-of-care capability (create/send and receive/validate) during the transition, while continuing to advance FHIR-based alternatives through discrete, testable implementation guidance.

title: Comment from Sokol-Hessner, Lauge, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
As a practicing physician, I strongly urge ONC/ASTP to retain federal certification criteria requiring usability testing and safety-focused design practices for electronic health records (EHRs).

EHR usability is not an abstract concern, it is a patient safety issue. There is substantial evidence that poorly designed interfaces -- including confusing displays of information and sub-optimally designed workflows -- contribute to medication errors, incorrect orders, documentation mistakes, and diagnostic delays. The preventable harm that follows is inexcusable.

Certification requirements that include structured usability testing and safety practices are not administrative luxuries; they are safeguards that protect patients and clinicians. Eliminating these requirements in the name of burden reduction risks increasing downstream burden in the form of adverse events, rework, malpractice exposure, and clinician burnout.

Safety and burden reduction are not mutually exclusive. Thoughtful usability standards can and should advance both. Other high-risk industries maintain long-standing safety-oriented design requirements because the stakes demand it. Healthcare should be no different.

I respectfully urge ONC/ASTP to preserve usability and safety-focused certification requirements to ensure that EHR systems support, rather than undermine, safe patient care.

title: Comment from Boone, Keith, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
As with all of my comments, please note that these are my personal opinions, and do not necessarily represent those of my employer (Audacioous Inquiry), or its parent company, PointClickCare.

I appreciate the care with which ASTP/ONC made in putting together this rule and the preceding withdrawal of the remaining HTI2 proposals, if the timing was a bit challenging as usual, dropping just before the holidays.

I have very few concerns about what was removed, although I am aware that many on the patient advocacy front have deeper concerns.  I think a better job could have been done in prefatory content explaining the rational for removals, and better highlighting that removal of some of these criteria represents "mission accomplished", and the expectation that having delivered these features which are widely used in certified product (or removing features that few actually attest to), the results would have less impact than would be expected based on a pure regulatory requirement.

My general sense is that the removed criteria either represent a capability that is unlikely to disappear from the market due to wide adoption, or was never really adopted in the first place.

My largest complaint is about the cost estimate on reviewers in #HTI5:

ONC says: "We estimate those costs to be $284,132 in total for an estimated 644 reviewers"

There are about 500 vendors with certified products.  More than half will review this rule.  In addition to developers and associations, consumers of Health IT will also review the rule (Health Systems and Hospitals).  

Arguably there are at least 500 organizations reviewing the content of HTI5. Each of those organizations will have 2-3 people on the review.  #HTI5 is 65 pages long.  I'm going to say it took me 2 hours to review it, which is at least what a pro focused on it would spend.  Associations will have a tiger team of 5-8 people on it.  Review teams will meet for 30 minutes to plan their review. Then they will review #HTI5 for at least an hour, maybe two. Then they will meet again to discuss their results (another 30 minutes). Then someone gets to write a response (at least another 30 minutes, more like 60).

That's 3 hours, times 3 people, times 500 organizations or 4500 hours. The reviewers are executives mostly, or senior staffers. The balanced cost about $150/hr. That works out to $675K, not just under $300K. Using my simple figures, a generous underestimate, #HTI5's estimate of reviewer cost is LOW.

The savings impact of #HTI5 is likely overestimated.  ONC estimates a savings of $174,843,873 in the first year. However, I don't see that happening. The cost to certify product is NOT going to go down much. It's not like you get to throw away popular delivered features.  At least half the effort in certification is planning.  That effort will be reduced by maybe 20%.  The number of tests you certify to will go down significantly, so maybe you spend half the time certifying after #HTI5 as you did previously.

Assuming a vendor saves 100 hours in recertification under #HTI5 (team of 5 times 20 hours), and pays 5000 less for certification, you get a savings for recertifications of new software version (the bulk of the ongoing costs) of about 15K per product, times 717, or ~$11M a year. That's significantly less than $174M.  Where did ONC go wrong on these estimates in #HTI5?  I think they went wrong by assuming the savings was in development costs.  But the number of new product certifications requiring development of removed requirements is very low.

Most certifications are effectively "recertifications" of products which already had these features.  That's maintenance of the feature, rather than development and integration.  The latter cost was incurred the first time the feature was implemented and does not reoccur. 

HL7 had having multiple workgroups review #HTI5.  Patient empowerment just spent a whole quarter (1.5 hours) with 10 people reviewing it.  

I figure the cost on that meeting alone reviewing #HTI5 is about $3K, and that at least six other workgroups or committees (e.g. the PAC) will review.  Then these will undergo review by the PAC. That's 6% of ONC's estimated 284K for review of the regulation my $3K is probably an UNDERESTIMATE of cost.

I understand EHRA spent a similar amount of time on its review across multiple workgroups, and that professional societies are also spending a good bit of time on it.  I know of organization investing in many meetings with senior leaders over the course of 6 weeks.

title: Comment from Lawrence, Lindsay, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text: Audit trail and preservation standards need to be strengthened not deregulated.

title: Comment from Tuttle, Craig, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Dear ASTP representatives:

I am writing today as both a patient (consumer of health care) and as an attorney assisting patients with injury claims relating to their medical care. I am also Chair of the Ohio Patient Safety Alliance, a group of attorneys advocating for safer medical care for patients of Ohio's hospitals and medical providers. 

The integrity and security of electronic health information is vital to me and my organization, so that it can promote patient safety. This is also important to me personally, as it relates to my own medical care and records. 

While I support the idea of enhancing interoperability, I have significant concerns that the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability (HTI-5) proposed rule risks undermining these values by modifying certification requirements before modernizing foundational data standards.  

The integrity of a medical record is only as reliable as its audit trail, something I deal with on a daily basis as I try to help patients understand "what happened?" An updated audit trail standard is necessary to ensure the security of EHI, to verify its integrity, and to reduce billing fraud. The current standard, ASTM E2147-18, is outdated and insufficient for the modern clinical environment. Since 2018, the use and content of medical records have evolved dramatically and rapidly. If the guardrails in place are removed, or decertification is pursued, before first defining how data is created, collected, and preserved in a usable, searchable, and sharable format, we are putting patient safety at risk, and are jeopardizing the the trust that patients have in our healthcare system.  

Medical records must remain a reliable source of information about medical care, and therefore, I implore HHS to prioritize a revision of the audit trail standard that includes: 
--AI, Medical Device, and Messaging Integration, and which explicitly captures the activities of AI algorithms, connected medical devices, and professional messaging to ensure clinical recommendations and data creation are transparent and traceable. These professional messages regarding patient care are a critical part of the practice of medicine, and patients are entitled to these communications between providers, just as they were prior to the advent of electronic medical records.
--Software Identification and Interoperability, wherein audit trails identify the specific software used in patient care. These software programs must also be interoperable for safe functioning within facilities and for sharing information in compliance withTEFCA.
--Granular Metadata and Fraud Prevention, where standards must require the collection of precise data elements, including user roles and detailed activity logs (e.g., "copy-paste," "copy forwards," and "view-only" access). Audit trails that are transparent and reveal corrections, deletions, or overwrites are essential to prevent the fraudulent practices that cost billions of dollars annually.
--Patient Access: Even still, many patients do not have access to their EHI, despite such access being required by law. These troubling gaps in availability must be addressed, so that patients can remain informed about their healthcare. 

The move toward interoperability requires that data be not only shareable but also preserved. Data retention must be well defined so that information remains available while patient care, investigation, or even litigation, is ongoing. This data must be retained to remain in compliance with state and federal regulations. I strongly urge HHS to prohibit "ephemeral messaging" or any automatic destruction of data. Data must be retained in a readable, reproducible format for as long as it remains in use, and must be available to the patient as part of the medical record.

While I agree that we must establish clear, shared definitions for terms like ePHI, EHI, Generative AI, and Synthetic Data, these standards for data preservation and the ability to reproduce data must be updated, codified, and enforced prior to any deregulation is considered. Without taking this step, this has the real potential to actually facilitate fraudulent use of electronic health records upon removal of the guardrails in place. 

Audit trails are essential to billing integrity, patient trust, and legal accountability. These are not optional tools within the EMR. I ask that HHS hold off on the proposed certification changes until a modern and robust audit and preservation standard is finalized.  

Thank you for your important work on this issue, and for considering the implication of the proposals.
 
Sincerely,
Craig S. Tuttle

title: Comment from Watts, Chloe , HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
I support ONC’s goals of reducing unnecessary regulatory burden, fostering innovation, and advancing interoperable access to electronic health information, including the long‑term vision of a FHIR‑enabled, API‑driven ecosystem. However, I am concerned that this proposed rule removes critical certification, reporting, and public health infrastructure safeguards before replacement capabilities are operationally ready at scale.
FHIR‑based access alone does not equate to readiness for downstream use cases such as public health surveillance, reporting, and emergency response. The success of a FHIR‑forward strategy depends not only on data availability, but also on the readiness of receiving systems, governance, workforce capacity, and operational workflows. Many state, local, territorial, and tribal public health agencies are not yet positioned to reliably receive and operationalize FHIR‑based reporting at scale, and existing CDA‑based or hybrid pipelines remain essential for continuity.
The proposed removal or weakening of public health certification criteria and reporting transparency risks data gaps, reduced situational awareness, and uneven impacts across jurisdictions with differing levels of technical capacity. Modernization should not come at the expense of continuity, particularly during public health emergencies.
I am also concerned about the implicit retirement or de‑emphasis of the CDC’s Vocabulary Access and Distribution System (PHIN/VADS). PHIN/VADS remains a critical operational dependency for jurisdictions and immunization information systems, and is relied upon regularly—often daily—to guide implementation decisions, resolve vocabulary questions, align reporting expectations, and support active technical discussions with providers and vendors. Retiring or allowing PHIN/VADS‑dependent workflows to atrophy before a fully operational, authoritative, and jurisdiction‑ready substitute is available risks fragmentation, inconsistent interpretation of standards, and regression in public health reporting quality. PHIN/VADS or an equivalent service should remain available until states and public health agencies confirm that replacement solutions fully meet their technical and governance needs.
The proposed rule emphasizes access to electronic health information while reducing requirements that historically supported reliability, usability, safety, and accountability once data is exchanged. This shift from outcome assurance to access assurance introduces risk for both clinical care and public health, particularly for under‑resourced providers and jurisdictions that rely on certification as a baseline safeguard.
I urge ONC to adopt a phased, readiness‑based approach to deregulation. Removal of public health and reporting‑related certification requirements should be conditioned on demonstrated readiness and sustained operational capability. A minimal, enforceable public health reporting baseline should be maintained during the transition, along with limited transparency measures that assess whether public health data exchange is actually occurring, not merely whether APIs exist.
I support ONC’s modernization goals, but modernization must proceed in a way that preserves patient safety, public health continuity, and equity while acknowledging current operational realities.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment. This comment reflects my personal professional perspective and does not represent the views of my employer or any affiliated organization.

title: Comment from Ryan, Thomas, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
RE: Public Comment on Proposed Rule RIN 0955-AA09 (HTI-5)

Dear ASTP Representative:

I submit this comment as an attorney who has spent many years reviewing electronic medical records in matters involving patient safety. I am reviewing electronic medical records and audit trails at least once a week, or more.  My perspective is shaped not by theory, but by real-world experience with how electronic health information (EHI) functions in practice and the relationship between records, bills, and audit trails.  

At its core, this rule should be evaluated through one question: Does it strengthen or weaken a patient’s ability to access, review, challenge, understand, and trust their own medical record?

I support interoperability and modernization. Patients deserve seamless, secure access to their health information. However, I am concerned that modifying or removing certification criteria before modernizing audit trail and data preservation standards risks undermining the accuracy and reliability of the medical record itself.

The medical record serves as the foundation for diagnosis, treatment, billing, insurance determinations, disability evaluations, and care coordination. Its integrity depends on transparent audit functionality.  It is important to know what is in the medical record, but also when the information was entered, modified, or deleted. Without clear and updated standards governing how data is created, modified, stored, and exported, patients cannot be assured that their records are complete, accurate, or traceable.  

In my experience, audit trails are essential not only for accountability, but for patient safety. They reveal when entries are corrected, modified, overwritten, or copied forward. Often, these changes are appropriate and clinically justified. But without transparent and modern audit standards, patients and providers alike lack confidence in the integrity of the record.

Before certification guardrails are reduced, I respectfully urge HHS to prioritize modernization of audit trail standards to ensure:

1. Meaningful Traceability
Audit logs should capture clinically significant actions, including edits, overwrites, copy-forward functions, user roles, and access activity.

2. Inclusion of Emerging Technologies
If AI systems, connected medical devices, or provider messaging platforms influence patient care, those activities should be logged and preserved as electronic health information.

3. Durable Preservation
Clinically relevant communications and EHI should be retained in a readable, reproducible format consistent with federal and state retention requirements. Systems used in patient care should not allow automatic destruction of relevant clinical data.

4. Enforceable Patient Access
Despite existing federal law, many patients still face practical barriers in obtaining complete records. Regulatory reform should reinforce—not dilute—the enforceability of patient access rights under the 21st Century Cures Act.

I am particularly concerned about the proposed removal of certain certification criteria, including 45 C.F.R. §§ 170.315(d)(2), (d)(3), (d)(10), (b)(11), and (g)(3), without first establishing updated standards that ensure authentication integrity, audit transparency, and reliable EHI export.

Interoperability and regulatory streamlining are worthy goals. But deregulation should not precede the establishment of modern safeguards that protect the accuracy and completeness of the medical record.

Patients rely on their records as a source of truth. Federal health IT policy should ensure that those records remain transparent, durable, and trustworthy.  

Therefore, I respectfully oppose the proposed changes and deletion of the identified certification criteria, as doing so at this stage would weaken essential protections that ensure patients can access complete, accurate, and trustworthy medical records.  It would also create unnecessary patient safety risks by diminishing the safeguards that ensure the integrity, traceability, and reliability of electronic health information.  

Respectfully submitted,
Thomas P. Ryan
Cleveland, Ohio

The health centers we represent rely extensively on Certified Electronic Health Record Technology (CEHRT) and interconnected health information systems to deliver care across multi-site environments. Most operate with lean IT teams and limited in-house cybersecurity expertise, making vendor-based certification safeguards a critical component of their HIPAA Security Rule compliance strategy. Our member centers routinely manage sensitive protected health information across cloud-hosted platforms, revenue cycle vendors, pharmacy interfaces, health information exchanges, and third-party population health tools, creating a complex and highly interdependent digital environment.

Given this distributed technology architecture, the removal of federal privacy and security certification criteria would not reduce compliance obligations for health centers; rather, it would shift technical validation, due diligence, and risk assessment responsibilities onto organizations that do not control software design or development. For safety net providers operating on narrow margins, the loss of certification “floors” materially increases operational risk, financial exposure, and vulnerability to cybersecurity threats

title: Comment from Melewski, Ryan, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Comment of Ryan Melewski, Esq.
Submitted to: ASTP/ONC — Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions to Unleash Prosperity (HTI-5) Proposed Rule
Federal Register Docket: 2025-23896
Comment Period: December 29, 2025 – February 27, 2026

Dear ASTP/ONC Representatives,

I submit this comment as an Ohio attorney deeply committed to patient safety, accountability, and the integrity of electronic health information. I am writing as an individual, not on behalf of any organization, to express serious concerns about components of the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions to Unleash Prosperity (HTI-5) Proposed Rule.

At its core, the HTI-5 proposal aims to streamline and reduce burdens within the ONC Health IT Certification Program by eliminating or revising a significant number of certification criteria, and by resetting the regulatory scope for future interoperability standards. Among these changes are proposed removals of long-standing auditing and privacy/security functionality that has served as a safeguard for patients and clinicians.

Let me be clear: innovation cannot come at the expense of truth in the clinical record. An audit trail — the digital footprint of who accessed, created, edited, or deleted health data — is not an optional feature. It is the foundation of trust in electronic health information. Removing or weakening requirements for auditable events, audit reports, and auditable actions on health information would reduce transparency and remove essential tools for identifying errors, preventable harm, and potential fraud.

The proposed rule contemplates removing certification criteria in the Privacy and Security domain, including auditable events and tamper resistance (§ 170.315(d)(2)), audit report(s) (§ 170.315(d)(2)), and auditing actions on health information (§ 170.315(d)(10)). These capabilities are not antiquated burdens — they are critical to ensuring the security, accountability, and integrity of patient data in an era of increasing digital threats and data complexity.

Without reliable audit data, it becomes exponentially more difficult — if not impossible — to reconstruct what happened when a patient’s clinical information is altered. This is not hypothetical. Accurate audit trails are essential for clinicians to verify data used in diagnosis and treatment, for patients to understand their own care histories, and for regulatory and legal investigations that identify systemic risks and prevent future harm. Dismantling these safeguards shifts risk onto patients and erodes public confidence in health IT.

Furthermore, the HTI-5 rule should not proceed to remove fundamental certification protections before updated standards for data creation, preservation, and exchange are firmly in place. Interoperability that emphasizes data movement without protecting how that data is governed, audited, and preserved invites clinical and administrative chaos — exactly the opposite of what patients deserve.

My recommendations are straightforward:

Retain and reinforce audit trail certification criteria — including auditable events, audit reports, and audit trail functionality — until modernized standards are codified that preserve meaningful accountability.

Ensure definitions and standards for key EHI concepts (such as audit logs, tamper resistance, and traceability) are updated so that patients, clinicians, auditors, and regulators have consistent, enforceable expectations for how data is created, accessed, and shared.

Do not remove privacy and security safeguards until there are robust mechanisms to ensure that data integrity and patient access remain protected in all interoperable environments.

Recognize that deregulation must not equate to defanging the tools that reveal systemic risk, prevent harm, and protect taxpayers.

The promise of interoperability, and of AI-enabled innovation, is real. But innovation without accountability is not progress — it is peril. Public trust in healthcare data depends on transparency that cannot be achieved without meaningful audit requirements and clear standards that hold health IT to account.

I urge ASTP/ONC to reconsider any deregulatory action that would undermine the integrity, security, and accountability of electronic health information. The stakes are too high — patient safety, clinical validity, and the basic right of every person to trust their health information depend on it.

Respectfully submitted,
Ryan Melewski, Esq.

title: Comment on HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
The retention of accurate data is critically important. Retention policies must be clearly defined to ensure that information remains accessible for the duration of patient care, investigations, or litigation holds, and to maintain full compliance with applicable state and federal regulations.
We strongly urge HHS to prohibit the use of “ephemeral messaging” or any system that permits automatic deletion of data. All data must be preserved in a readable and reproducible format.
We support the development of clear, uniform definitions for terms such as ePHI, EHI, Generative AI, and Synthetic Data. However, before any deregulation of certification is considered, data preservation standards — including requirements for reproducibility — must first be modernized, codified, and meaningfully enforced. Eliminating the existing framework without implementing essential safeguards would perpetuate unsafe practices and facilitate improper billing conduct. Sections 170.315(d)(10), 170.315(d)(2), 170.315(d)(3), 170.315(b)(11), and 170.315(g)(3) should not be removed. 
Robust audit trails are fundamental to patient safety and system accountability. In the absence of clearly defined and enforceable standards, the integrity of electronic health information cannot be assured. This gap would expose patients to significant clinical risk and leave taxpayers vulnerable to fraud and abuse.

title: Comment from Myriddian LLC, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
As a team that participated in the pilot testing, we respectfully submit that the current FHIR resources for cancer reporting are not yet ready for broad operational deployment. While the potential of FHIR‑based reporting is clear, these resources require substantially more validation, real‑world testing, and implementation experience across diverse clinical environments to ensure accuracy, reliability, and consistency.

Proposed HTI-5 rule "§170.315(f)(4) certification criterion transmission to cancer registries" removing certification criteria for the current, established reporting formats before widespread readiness of FHIR-based approaches would significantly disrupt cancer surveillance workflows. Such disruption would:

* Impair the continuity and completeness of cancer case reporting
* Delay the transmission of case data to public health agencies
* Negatively affect reporting timeliness, which is foundational for maintaining accurate, up‑to‑date cancer incidence rates
* Undermine the ability to monitor cancer trends, evaluate disparities, support research participation, and inform evidence‑based policy decisions

Timely case reporting is essential for the accuracy of national cancer incidence rates. Any slowdown in reporting caused by immature standards, fragmented adoption, or gaps in certified technology will directly affect the quality and timeliness of cancer registry data.

For these reasons, we strongly recommend maintaining the existing certification criteria until the FHIR resources have undergone broader testing, demonstrated operational stability, and achieved sufficient adoption across vendors and healthcare systems.

Dr. Vijay Medithi
SVP, Disease Surveillance

title: Comment from Massachusetts Health Data Consortium, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Dear Dr. Kean,

The Massachusetts Health Data Consortium appreciates the opportunity to comment on the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability ASTP-ONC Deregulatory Actions to Unleash Prosperity (HTI‑5) Proposed Rule. We hope our insights and perspectives are useful as ASTP-ONC advances its commitment to modernizing the Health Information Technology Certification Program to reduce low-value regulatory burden and advance API-forward interoperability.

MHDC strongly supports ASTP/ONC's vision of this modernization. Moreover, we aim to help ASTP-ONC realize its goals of clarifying and strengthening information-blocking policy, so that access to electronic health data works not just in principle, but in real-life practice.

Indeed, as interoperability continues to evolve from document-centric exchange toward API-forward workflows, the central challenge is no longer technical feasibility, but operational reliability at scale. So, MHDC believes HTI-5's success will hinge on whether the final rule will provide enough clarity and continuity to enable predictable, secure, reusable integration across the ecosystem as certification requirements are modernized.

For over four decades, MHDC has served as a trusted, neutral convener that advances health data exchange, interoperability, and administrative simplification across Massachusetts and nationally. As a result of our merger with the New England Healthcare Exchange Network, MHDC now supports secure data exchange and automation for over 4,000 health professionals across more than 200 organizations.

Our recommendations draw on MHDC's long history of successfully engaging payers, providers, vendors, and exchanges in real-world environments. Most importantly, all our comments aim to support ASTP-ONC's objectives by highlighting practical considerations to strengthen the operational reality of interoperability. Our general comments and recommendations are below; more-extensive details are in the attached PDF.

Based on MHDC’s extensive experience, collaborations, and a careful review of implementations and outcomes, we conclude that burden reduction will succeed only if it decreases total friction across the ecosystem - not if it shifts cost, risk, or bespoke integration work onto providers, payers, exchanges, and implementers.

To ensure that well-intentioned deregulation succeeds in strengthening the operational reality of interoperability, MHDC therefore recommends that the HTI-5 final rule should
-Prevent burden shifting by pairing removed or revised regulations with clear crosswalks, responsibility assignment, and post-final-rule evaluation
-Preserve an interoperability floor during the transition to APIs, so that baseline exchange remains predictable and reusable
-Discourage network-by-network API arrangements and require predictable, reusable integration patterns so API exchange can scale without bespoke builds
-Maintain trust through minimum, testable security expectations to avoid gaps as legacy criteria are removed
-Define minimum, testable API security baselines and avoid security gaps during the transition from legacy criteria
-Provide an executable implementation roadmap with timelines and "removed to replaced by" crosswalks to reduce uncertainty, renegotiation, and rework
-Assign clear responsibility to developers for any displaced capability or safeguard, and commit to post-final-rule evaluation to verify burden is reduced, not shifted.
-Include worked examples for high-impact use cases to make the final rule executable in practice
-Retain a small set of meaningful interoperability performance signals so reduced reporting does not reduce accountability

MHDC also urges ASTP-ONC to address operational barriers that most-directly impede scalable exchange in practice, particularly for payer-provider workflows like prior authorization and quality exchange. MHDC therefore recommends that the final rule should:
-Prevent "pay-to-play" access to electronic health information by establishing enforceable guardrails on fees and licensing terms, with clear examples of prohibited practices and clarified enforcement expectations
-Make information-blocking updates administrable with practical examples, checklists, and workable documentation expectations that discourage strategic delay and procedural blocking
-Preserve multi-path exchange and choice by ensuring that TEFCA is effective where it fits, without becoming a ceiling that slows adoption of technically superior API-based exchange for high-volume workflows

We urge ASTP-ONC to ensure HTI-5 strengthens the operational reality of interoperability by preserving trust, preventing burden shifting, discouraging pay-to-play EHI access, and offering a clear, executable transition roadmap and input for HTI-6, as appropriate. MHDC is grateful for ASTP-ONC's leadership and for this opportunity to provide comments.

Respectfully submitted,
Denny Brennan
Executive Director

title: Comment from Tittle, Allen, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
To the Representatives of ASTP/ONC:

I am a plaintiff medical malpractice trial attorney. For over fifteen years, I have represented catastrophically injured patients and families in cases where the truth depended entirely on the integrity of the electronic medical record (EMR). I submit this comment in strong opposition to the deregulatory proposals in HTI-5 (90 Fed. Reg. 60970 (Dec. 29, 2025)) 

From a courtroom perspective, what is being described as “burden reduction” is the removal of the only reliable mechanisms that allow courts and patients to detect fabrication, backdating, selective deletion, and undisclosed post-event alteration of medical records.

In case after case, federally required audit trails have revealed:

- Operative reports signed before surgery began.

- Care plans created after a patient’s death but printed as if contemporaneous.

- Vital signs copied shift-to-shift without new measurement.

- Medication records charted as given after the required time window.

- Progress notes entered after discharge to retroactively justify care.

- Post-death access to medical records that defendants fought to conceal.

These facts were discoverable only because certified systems generated tamper-evident audit logs. Without mandatory audit functionality, these events would have remained invisible.

HTI-5 projects approximately $1.53 billion in cost savings to developers. That figure does not account for the cost to patients when falsified documentation goes undetected, or to courts when multi-day forensic inspections are required to uncover records that should have been produced.

Modern EMR systems now include AI-generated notes, copy-forward automation, predictive documentation tools, multi-platform data integration, and API-driven access. Interoperability without traceability creates risk. If certification requirements are removed before a modern, enforceable audit standard is adopted, accountability will weaken at the very moment documentation systems are becoming more automated and more complex.

Before removing certification safeguards, ASTP/ONC should:

Finalize and codify a modern audit trail standard that:

Flags AI-generated entries;

Identifies copy-forward source and propagation;

Captures pre-documentation and post-discharge/post-death access;

Requires retention and production in native, machine-readable format;

Prohibits automatic destruction of care-related data.

Require audit continuity across all platforms involved in a patient’s care.

Codify that audit trails are non-privileged and subject to full patient access.

Efficiency cannot come at the expense of integrity. In litigation, audit logs are often the only mechanism preventing falsified documentation from becoming the accepted record of care.

I respectfully urge ASTP/ONC to pause the proposed deregulatory removals and implement a comprehensive, enforceable audit framework before eliminating existing safeguards.

Respectfully submitted,
Allen Tittle
Tittle & Perlmuter
Cleveland, Ohio

title: Comment from CUNNINGHAM, SABRINA , HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Dear ASTP Representatives:
This matter is significant to me, professionally and personally, as well as to others for whom I bear responsibility. As the healthcare proxy and primary caregiver for both of my parents during their battles with cancer and heart disease, I relied heavily on their medical records to make informed decisions on their behalf. That reliance underscored for me the essential role that trustworthy medical documentation plays in patient care and decision-making. The same reliance exists in my role as a parent.
Professionally, I have spent years working as part of a team responsible for making decisions that can significantly affect the course of individuals’ lives—decisions grounded in and guided by the medical record. Through this work, I witness daily the critical importance of maintaining electronic medical records (EMRs) that are accurate, complete, and dependable. 

I also have years of first-hand professional experience with the essential and interdependent role of the audit trail in the EMR environment. I have observed how the absence of a modernized and consistently applied audit trail standard limits the EMR’s ability to fulfill its purpose—namely a verifiable record. While EMR systems have evolved significantly in real-world healthcare practice, including the implementation of AI integration, audit trail standards have not progressed at the same pace, creating gaps that undermine transparency, accountability, and trust.

I support the goal of enhancing interoperability for information sharing and patient safety specifically. However, I share the concerns of others who have deep concerns that the proposed Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability (HTI-5) rule, puts the goal of patient safety and trust in our healthcare system at risk by modifying certification requirements before modernizing the foundation-how data is created, collected, and preserved in a usable, and shareable format. To borrow a phrase-the house won’t fall if the bones are good.  

There exists a concurrent need for revision of the audit trail standard. ASTM E2147-18, is outdated and insufficient for today’s clinical and technological environment. Since 2018, the use, structure, and content of medical records have evolved dramatically. To support and not thwart an accurate, complete, reliable, and verifiable medical record, a new audit trail standard should include: AI, Medical Device, and Messaging Integration; Software Identification and Interoperability; Granular Metadata and Fraud Prevention; Standards must mandate the collection of precise data elements, including user roles and detailed activity logs (e.g., "copy-paste," "copy forwards," and "view-only" access). Audit trails that are transparent and reveal corrections, deletions, or overwrites, including the specific data that was changed, are essential to prevent the fraudulent practices that cost billions of dollars annually. 

Patient Access: We must address the reality that many patients still do not have access to their 
complete EMR, despite such transparency being required by law. 

Preservation Over Ephemerality- The move toward interoperability requires that data be not only shareable but also durable. Data retention must be clearly defined so that information is available as long as patient care, investigations, or litigation holds are ongoing, and to remain in compliance with state and federal regulations. I strongly urge HHS to prohibit "ephemeral messaging" or any automatic destruction of data. information regarding the decisions underlying their care and treatment.

Audit trails are foundational—not optional—to billing integrity, patient trust, and legal accountability. We must define what a "trustworthy record" is before we change the rules on how those records are certified. I ask that HHS pause the proposed certification changes until a modern, robust audit and preservation standard is finalized.

Thank you for your time, attention, and your commitment to the health and safety of the American public.

Respectfully, 
Sabrina Cunningham

comment_text:
I appreciate ONC/ASTP’s continued leadership in advancing interoperability, improving data liquidity, and modernizing the Health IT Certification Program through the HTI-5 proposed rule. The emphasis on standards-based exchange, reduced burden where appropriate, and preparation for emerging technologies represents meaningful progress toward a more connected and innovative digital health ecosystem. These efforts are essential to improving care coordination and enabling the next generation of health IT capabilities.

At the same time, health IT plays a central role in care delivery, and its design, functionality, and safeguards have direct implications for patient safety. Research, real-world safety events, and federal enforcement actions have demonstrated that deficiencies in usability, auditability, authentication, clinical decision support transparency, and quality management can contribute to medication errors, wrong-patient events, failures in care transitions, and an inability to investigate and correct system-related harm. The Health IT Certification Program has served as a critical national safety floor, ensuring that baseline protections are present across products and vendors. Certification criteria addressing usability testing, audit logging, clinical decision support transparency, authentication, and systematic quality management were adopted specifically to mitigate known and documented patient safety risks. These criteria help ensure accountability, transparency, and systematic risk mitigation in systems that clinicians and patients rely upon every day.
Based on analysis of the HTI-5 proposed removals, the following criteria represent important patient safety protections:

Tier 1: Critical Safety Risk Criteria
•170.315(b)(11) – Decision Support Interventions (source attribution and transparency)
•170.315(g)(3) – Safety-Enhanced Design (usability testing and human factors processes)
•170.315(b)(2) – Clinical Information Reconciliation and Incorporation
•170.315(d)(2) – Auditable Events and Tamper-Resistance
Tier 2: High Safety Risk Criteria
•170.315(d)(1) – Authentication, Access Control, and Authorization
•170.315(g)(4) – Quality Management System
•170.315(d)(10) – Auditing Actions on Health Information
•170.315(b)(1) – Transitions of Care
•170.315(d)(13) – Multi-Factor Authentication
Tier 3: Moderate Safety Concern Criteria
•170.315(a)(9) – Clinical Decision Support
•170.315(d)(3) – Audit Reports
•170.315(d)(5) – Automatic Access Time-Out
•170.315(d)(8) – Integrity

These criteria collectively address core safety domains, including usability, clinical workflow reliability, auditability and traceability, authentication and access control, clinical decision support transparency, and systematic quality management. Removing these protections would weaken the certification program’s role as a safety assurance mechanism and reduce accountability for ensuring that health IT systems incorporate baseline safety safeguards.

Rather than removing these criteria, I respectfully recommend that ONC/ASTP maintain them in their current form or optimize and modernize them as needed to align with evolving technologies and care delivery models. Optimization could include streamlining testing processes, harmonizing with existing standards, or clarifying requirements—but eliminating these criteria entirely risks removing foundational safeguards without evidence that the underlying safety risks have been eliminated or mitigated through superior alternative mechanisms.

The Health IT Certification Program has played a critical role in advancing both interoperability and patient safety. As ONC/ASTP continues to modernize the program, preserving core safety-focused criteria will help ensure that innovation and interoperability advance alongside, not at the expense of, patient safety. I appreciate the opportunity to provide input and thank ONC/ASTP for its continued commitment to improving the safety and effectiveness of health IT.

title: Comment from Gotwalt, Aaron, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
We write in support of the HTI-5 Proposed Rule and offer one targeted recommendation. We are health IT developers and entrepreneurs with twenty-five years of experience building clinical data exchange and interoperability systems.

ASTP/ONC proposes to expand the definitions of "access" and "use" in § 171.102 to include automated means, including autonomous AI systems. I support this proposal and recommend one addition: clarify that "use" encompasses the automated reconciliation and deduplication of electronic health information from multiple sources.

The Problem

The access problem is substantially solved. FHIR APIs are deployed, TEFCA is operational, and data flows between systems at increasing volume. But a well-documented gap persists between *receiving* data and *using* it.

The Sequoia Project's Data Usability Workgroup (486 participants from 391 organizations) has systematically documented this: duplicate records that cannot be automatically deduplicated, conflicting medication lists across coding systems, and no mechanism for computational resolution. When a physician receives a fifty-page C-CDA from an outside organization, the practical result is that the data is skimmed, ignored, or manually re-entered. KLAS Research confirms that many organizations connected through exchange networks are "not actively consuming outside data." Connection does not equal use.

HTI-5 implicitly acknowledges this by removing the Clinical Information Reconciliation and Incorporation criterion (§ 170.315(b)(2)), which required only manual display-and-select. That criterion failed. But nothing in the current proposal recognizes automated reconciliation as a dimension of "use."

Why It Matters for Information Blocking

An actor that provides technically compliant data, _i.e._ a valid C-CDA or a conformant FHIR response in a format that resists automated reconciliation with competing systems is creating friction that discourages switching and inhibits competition. This is precisely the behavior the Cures Act targeted and that HTI-5's revisions to the Infeasibility Exception (§ 171.204(a)(3)) and Manner Exception (§ 171.301(a)) aim to curtail.

Clarifying that "use" includes reconciliation gives ASTP/ONC an enforcement tool against this pattern. This is consistent with EO 14267 (Reducing Anti-Competitive Regulatory Barriers) and HTI-5's deregulatory posture. It also aligns with the preamble's "FHIR-Forward Future" vision acknowledging that complementary "successor technology or standards" (per the Cures Act, § 170.404) will be needed to provide capabilities FHIR alone does not offer.

Proposed Text

Addition to § 171.102 [new language in brackets]:

> Use means the ability of electronic health information, once accessed or exchanged, to be applied toward its intended purpose. This includes the ability to use electronic health information through automated means, including by autonomous artificial intelligence systems [and by systems that computationally reconcile, deduplicate, or merge electronic health information from multiple sources].

This creates no new certification criteria, mandates no specific technology, and imposes no additional compliance burden on any actor. It provides interpretive clarity for enforcement: practices that unreasonably frustrate automated reconciliation can be evaluated under the information blocking framework.

Access without usability is not meaningful access. We urge ASTP/ONC to make this explicit.

Respectfully submitted,

Aaron Gotwalt (Former CTO of Evernow and Doctrly, San Francisco, CA.)
and
James R. Thompson, D.Phil. (CTO of Morf Health, New York, NY.)

title: Comment on HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
We support the direction of the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability (HTI-5) proposed rule and its focus on modernizing and streamlining the federal health IT regulatory framework. In particular, we appreciate the intent to reset the scope of the ONC Health IT Certification Program by removing duplicative or outdated requirements, while establishing a stronger and more practical foundation for FHIR-based API capabilities going forward.

HTI-5 represents an important opportunity to reduce unnecessary compliance burden while reinforcing the core policy objectives of interoperability and patient access. We appreciate ASTP/ONC’s approach to advancing a more durable and future-ready regulatory foundation that supports innovation, competition, and responsible next-generation interoperability solutions.

However, we are concerned that maintaining the requirement in 45 CFR 170.404(b)(2) to publicly publish a list of Service Base URLs - associated with the certification criteria in § 170.315(g)(10)(viii)(B) - inadvertently functions as a proxy for competitively sensitive commercial data, creating unintended market externalities that undermine the competitive dynamics ASTP/ONC intends to promote. This requirement has the practical effect of disclosing both a developer’s customer footprint and the identity of those specific customers. For small and startup developers of certified health IT, this creates meaningful commercial risk and provides a mechanism for market distortion. 

When transparency requirements effectively signal customer footprint and market position, they can inadvertently shape competitive behavior in ways that favor incumbents and undermine new entrants. In other regulated industries, disclosure regimes intended to promote fairness and consumer benefit have sometimes been leveraged by dominant incumbents to monitor small rivals and disadvantage new entrants - such as the airline industry’s Computer Reservations Systems (CRS), where real-time pricing visibility enabled market consolidation rather than healthy competition. In a market where the same dominant providers have maintained entrenched positions for decades, the continued public disclosure of Service Base URLs provides incumbents with a strategic roadmap to identify and target emerging competitors. 

For small businesses and new entrants attempting to scale, this transparency creates an asymmetric risk: it allows dominant entities to deploy defensive, loss-leader tactics - such as loss-leader pricing - that can neutralize a rival before it reaches critical mass. Eliminating this requirement is essential to ensure that mandated public disclosures do not serve as a low-cost market surveillance tool for incumbents. Protecting the strategic growth of new entrants is vital to reducing the barriers to entry in the market and fostering the genuine competition this legislation seeks to unlock. 
From a practical standpoint, third-party developers and integration partners rarely build viable businesses on top of platforms with small customer footprints. If public Service Base URL publication becomes a proxy for customer scale, it may reinforce a 'rich get richer' dynamic, steering ecosystem development toward incumbents and away from innovators - counter to ASTP/ONC’s goals of competition, interoperability, and long-term market dynamism.

We recommend ASTP/ONC modify § 170.315(g)(10)(viii)(B) to establish minimum qualifications (e.g., based on customer count and/or customer size) that a developer of certified health IT must meet prior to being required to publish Service Base URL documentation publicly, in a similar vein to ONC's implementation of the Insights Conditions requirement. This approach would preserve transparency objectives while protecting the growth and competitive viability of new entrants during their most vulnerable scaling phases and avoiding a compliance mechanism that inadvertently functions as a market-disadvantaging disclosure mandate.

title: Comment from Keyes, Bart, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
My law firm regularly represents patients and families in medical negligence cases. Our work holds negligent providers accountable and protects patient safety for the whole community.

I am concerned that the Health Data, Technology and Interoperability (HTI-5) proposed rule risks unintentionally impairing patient’s ability to access all of their data by modifying certification requirements before modernizing data standards.  We routinely use audit trail data to uncover medical data that was added to, modified or deleted from the original data.  At times, these changes to the medical record are routine and consistent with standards of care. Yet there are many times when we find that this altering of data resulted in preventable catastrophic harm or, even worse, were attempts to conceal conduct that must be revealed so that it can be prevented in the future. The integrity of the medical record is only as reliable as its audit trail.  An updated audit trail standard is required for the security of EHI to verify its integrity and curtail billing fraud.  The current standard, ASTM E2147-18, is outdated.  Since 2018 the use and content of medical records have evolved dramatically.  If we move forward with the decertification or removal of functional guardrails without first defining how data is created, collected, and preserved in a usable, searchable, and shareable format, we invite patient harm and compromise the trustworthiness of our healthcare system.   

We must establish clear, shared definitions for terms like ePHI, EHI, Generative AI and Synthetic Data.  But, these standards for data preservation and the ability to reproduce data must be updated, codified and enforced before deregulation of certification is considered.  Taking away this system without first putting critical safeguards in place ensures continued unsafe practices and insidious billing fraud.    I oppose the deletions of sections 170.315(d)(10), 170.315(d)(2), 170.315(d)(3), 170.315(b)(11) and 170.315(g)(3). I'm also deeply concerned with the safety implication of failing to define provider chat platforms as Electronic Health Information in section 170.102. Audit trails are essential to patient safety and accountability.  Without well-defined standards it will be impossible to ensure the integrity of electronic health information leaving consumers open to clinical dangers while leaving taxpayers vulnerable to fraud.

title: Comment from Radiology Partners, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Radiology Partners appreciates the opportunity to submit comments on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions to Unleash Prosperity (HTI-5) Proposed Rule (RIN 0955-AA09) published by the Office of the Deputy Secretary and Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy (ASTP)/Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology (ONC) (collectively, ASTP/ONC), Department of Health and Human Services (HHS). 

Radiology Partners, through its affiliated practices, is a leading technology-enabled radiology practice in the United States, serving more than 3,400 healthcare facilities with high quality radiology and technology, including multiple artificial intelligence (AI) solutions. As a physician-led and physician-owned practice, our mission is to transform radiology by innovating across clinical value, technology, service and economics, while elevating the role of radiology and radiologists in healthcare. Radiology Partners, through its affiliated practices, provides consistent, differentiated care to patients, while delivering enhanced value to the hospitals, clinics, imaging centers and the referring physicians we serve.

Radiology Partners operates within thousands of distinct health systems and EMR environments, giving us extensive firsthand experience with the practical barriers that prevent seamless data exchange. Our integration with a wide range of hospitals and imaging centers may help provide perspective into how fragmented and unreliable interoperability remains, despite the existence of technical standards.

We address HHS’ HTI-5 Proposed Rule through the lens and experience of a radiology practice that depends on reliable data exchange to deliver timely, high-quality care. Although our examples come from radiology, the interoperability challenges we describe, such as limited data availability, inconsistent sharing practices, and organizational barriers, may be relevant to other specialties.

See attached comment letter.

Dear Sirs and Madams: 

I am an attorney who deals daily with electronic medical records, including audit data. We attempt to obtain, on behalf of our clients (patients and their families), the truth as to what happened. 

It is already not easy with the current rules and regulations. 

I have learned of HTI-5 and am very concerned as to what such deregulatory changes could mean in the search for the truth.

Megan J. Frantz Oldham

title: Comment from North Carolina Division of Public Health, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
North Carolina opposes the deregulatory approach outlined in HTI-5. Moving away from prescriptive standards risks creating ambiguity and inconsistency. Vendors may interpret the requirements differently which increases interoperability gaps. Safeguarding the integrity of people's health data and ensuring they receive consistent follow-up care is essential for maintaining public health trust and positive health outcomes. Deregulation could undermine interoperability and accountability in public health data exchange, such as electronic case reporting (eCR).

Similarly, While FHIR-based standards are attractive for clinical operability, they remain immature for complex public health workflows such as eCR. Transitioning to FHIR would impose significant resource burdens without clear evidence of improved outcomes for North Carolinians. FHIR also introduces security and privacy risks that require governance (which deregulation does not support).

These proposals create underfunded mandates for North Carolina's public health agency, which lacks the resources to absorb the costs of major technology transitions without dedicated federal support.

title: Comment from SPEKTRUM Health, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
To the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy and the Office of the National Coordinator for Health IT,

I am writing to express my strong opposition to several key provisions in the proposed rule, "Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions to Unleash Prosperity." While I support the goal of reducing administrative burden, the proposed removal of essential certification criteria threatens the safety, security, and equity of our national health IT infrastructure.

Specifically, I oppose the following actions:

Removal of Certification for Clinical Decision Support (CDS): Removing oversight for how technology assists clinicians in making life-saving decisions—including the removal of "model cards" for AI transparency—is a step backward. In an era of rapid AI integration, we need more transparency into algorithmic logic and bias, not less.

Elimination of Patient Safety and User-Centered Design Requirements: Rescinding requirements for safety-enhanced design (SED) shifts the burden of catching technology-induced errors onto overstressed healthcare providers. This increases the risk of medical errors and clinician burnout.

Rollback of Privacy and Security Guardrails: The proposal to remove or descope criteria related to multi-factor authentication (MFA), audit reports, and encryption is deeply concerning given the rise in healthcare cybersecurity breaches. Reducing federal security standards leaves patient data more vulnerable.

Reduction in Demographic Data Capture: I oppose the removal of standardized data elements for sex, sexual orientation, and gender identity. Comprehensive data is essential for identifying and addressing health disparities; removing these requirements hampers public health efforts and harms marginalized communities.

Conclusion: The proposed "reset" of the Certification Program prioritizes developer convenience over patient safety and data integrity. I urge the ASTP/ONC to maintain these critical guardrails to ensure that health IT remains a safe and reliable foundation for American healthcare.

Sincerely,

title: Comment from Association for Behavioral Health and Wellness, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
commenter_name: Association for Behavioral Health and Wellness
organization: Association for Behavioral Health and Wellness
comment_text:
Dear Assistant Secretary Keane: 

The Association for Behavioral Health and Wellness (ABHW) appreciates the opportunity to submit our recommendations to the Office of the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy/National Coordinator for Health Information Technology (ASTP/ONC) in response to Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions to Unleash Prosperity Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM or proposed rule).
ABHW is the national voice for payers managing behavioral health insurance benefits. Our member companies provide coverage to 200 million people in both the public and private sectors to treat mental health (MH), substance use disorders (SUDs), and other behaviors that impact health and wellness. Our organization aims to increase access, drive integration, support prevention, raise awareness, reduce stigma, and advance evidence-based treatment and quality outcomes. Furthermore, our policy work aims to ensure that physical and behavioral health care is integrated and coordinated. ABHW is committed to ensuring better outcomes for whole-person care for all individuals and communities. We believe access to comprehensive, evidence-based MH and SUD services is critical to enhancing patients’ health and overall well-being.
Our members strongly support efforts to modernize health IT infrastructure, improve interoperability, and reduce unnecessary administrative burden. We welcome ASTP/ONC’s deregulatory review and its focus on ensuring that certification requirements remain meaningful, efficient, and aligned with real-world implementation. At the same time, we urge caution when modifying privacy, security, and design-related certification criteria, particularly given the unique sensitivity of behavioral health information.

Please see our attached letter for more detailed information. 

Sincerely, 
 
Debbie Witchey, MHA 
President and CEO

title: Comment from Point of Care Partners, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Dear Tom, Keane, MD,

Point-of-Care Partners is an employee-owned health IT consulting firm with deep expertise in health data standards, value-based care and digital transformation. We offer actionable strategies, program guidance and implementation support to optimize interoperability and information management in a complex, rapidly evolving healthcare ecosystem. 

POCP supports ASTP/ONC’s goals and appreciates the opportunity to comment on the HTI-5 proposed rule. Our aim here is to provide experience- and evidence-based insights, analysis and recommendations to help HTI-5 achieve its intended outcomes without shifting cost, risk or operational burden onto providers, payers and implementers. 

To ensure that deregulatory actions fuel the growth and effectiveness of healthcare interoperability, POCP broadly recommends that the HTI-5 final rule: 

Prevent burden shifting and preserve accountability for displaced safeguards. 

-Maintain a clear interoperability floor during the transition to APIs. 

-Make API requirements executable through clear scope and conformance. 

-Ensure information blocking rules are administrable for automated exchange. 

-Preserve multi-path exchange (including TEFCA) without creating a ceiling. 

-Embed security, identity, and consent as core API requirements. 

-Retain guardrails for AI-enabled DSI to protect transparency and access to EHI. 

-Provide a phased, testable implementation timeline aligned with HTI-6. 

Recommendations based on real-world experience relating to cost, risk and scalability across payer, provider and vendor workflows. Together, they identify where HTI-5 can most-effectively bolster interoperability.
 
Prevent Burden Shifting and Preserve Operational Predictability 

-Pair removed/revised requirements with clear responsibility assignment, crosswalks, and post-final-rule evaluation. 

-Assign displaced safeguards and capabilities to Health IT developers. 

-Preserve developer testing as data exchange is now more network-to-network and automated. 

-Avoid ambiguity that results in ad hoc integrations or one-off arrangements. 

 
Preserve an Interoperability Floor During the API Transition 

- Define a minimum interoperability floor for all certified products. 

- Outline the path for replacing certification criteria with FHIR-based API requirements. 

-Continue support for multistakeholder standards development (e.g., HL7 Da Vinci, FAST). 

-Avoid single-connection or bilateral integration models impeding scalable exchange. 

-Make API Requirements Executable 

-Clearly scope API requirements and conformance expectations. 

-Do not require support for undefined future capabilities. 

-Promote predictable, reusable patterns to reduce custom integration burden. 

-Make Information-Blocking Rules Administrable and Enforceable 

-Provide practical examples and workable documentation expectations. 

-Curb procedural and strategic obstacles to automated, standards-based exchange.  

-Align enforcement expectations with machine-to-machine access patterns. 

-Preserve Multi-Path Exchange (Including TEFCA) 

-Enable TEFCA where appropriate without making it a ceiling for API-based exchange. 

-Clarify expectations for TEFCA participants while supporting API-forward workflows. 

-Encourage technically superior or operationally appropriate exchange mechanisms.  

-Security of Health Information and Trust 

-Embed interoperable authentication/authorization/identity/consent approaches. 

-Operationalize HL7 FAST IGs for security, identity and testing at scale. 

-Streamline certification to reduce fragmentation and build cross-sector trust. 

-Certification and Decision-Support Interventions 

-Retain governance and transparency for AI-enabled DSIs. 

-Ensure testing scenarios reflect actual clinical and operational risk. 

-Prevent DSIs from interfering with EHI access for patient care. 

Implementation and Timing 

-Publish a phased transition timeline with clear implementation milestones. 

-Align HTI-5 and HTI-6 to minimize uncertainty and duplicative investment. 

 
POCP serves as Program Managers for multistakeholder initiatives, including HL7 Da Vinci and FAST. Our experience implementing digital health standards across diverse environments allows us to assess HTI-5’s real-world impacts across stakeholders. 

POCP encourages continued collaboration between ASTP/ONC, healthcare organizations, health plans, technology vendors, and other stakeholders to accelerate progress. Working together, we can unlock the full potential of health data exchange, enabling providers to make well-informed decisions, empowering patients to actively participate in their care and, ultimately, leading to a more efficient, effective system for all. 

We welcome further discussion on our recommendations.

Sincerely, 

Anthony Scheuth, CEO, Point of Care Partners

title: Comment from Rivera, Joy, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
I appreciate ONC/ASTP’s ongoing leadership in driving interoperability, strengthening data fluidity, and updating the Health IT Certification Program through the HTI‑5 proposed rule. The continued focus on standards-based exchange, burden reduction where appropriate, and readiness for emerging technologies reflects meaningful forward movement toward a more connected, efficient, and innovative digital health ecosystem. These efforts remain essential for enhancing care coordination and enabling the next generation of health IT capabilities.
At the same time, health IT is deeply embedded in the delivery of care, and its design, functionality, and safeguards directly shape patient safety outcomes. Evidence from research, real-world safety incidents, and federal enforcement actions has consistently shown that gaps in usability, auditability, authentication, clinical decision support transparency, and quality management can contribute to issues such as medication errors, wrong‑patient events, breakdowns during care transitions, and limited ability to investigate or remediate system-related harm. The Health IT Certification Program has historically served as an important national baseline for safety, ensuring foundational protections are consistently present across vendors and products. Requirements around usability evaluation, audit logging, CDS transparency, authentication, and structured quality management were established specifically to address well‑documented risks. These criteria collectively reinforce accountability, visibility, and systematic risk management in systems clinicians and patients rely on every day.
After reviewing the proposed HTI‑5 removals, the following criteria reflect significant patient safety protections:
Tier 1: Critical Safety Risk Criteria

170.315(b)(11) – Decision Support Interventions (source attribution and transparency)
170.315(g)(3) – Safety-Enhanced Design (usability testing and human factors processes)
170.315(b)(2) – Clinical Information Reconciliation and Incorporation
170.315(d)(2) – Auditable Events and Tamper-Resistance

Tier 2: High Safety Risk Criteria

170.315(d)(1) – Authentication, Access Control, and Authorization
170.315(g)(4) – Quality Management System
170.315(d)(10) – Auditing Actions on Health Information
170.315(b)(1) – Transitions of Care
170.315(d)(13) – Multi-Factor Authentication

Tier 3: Moderate Safety Concern Criteria

170.315(a)(9) – Clinical Decision Support
170.315(d)(3) – Audit Reports
170.315(d)(5) – Automatic Access Time-Out
170.315(d)(8) – Integrity

Taken together, these criteria address essential safety domains such as usability and workflow reliability, auditability and traceability, authentication and access control, clinical decision support transparency, and structured quality management. Removing them would diminish the certification program’s role as a safety assurance mechanism and reduce the industry-wide accountability needed to ensure that baseline safeguards remain consistently implemented.
Instead of eliminating these protections, I respectfully recommend that ONC/ASTP retain them—either in their existing form or with updates that modernize and streamline them to reflect new technologies and care models. Improvements could include aligning requirements with established standards, simplifying testing procedures, or clarifying expectations. However, removing these criteria outright would eliminate foundational protections without clear evidence that the underlying safety risks have been resolved or replaced by stronger alternatives.
The Health IT Certification Program has long supported both interoperability and patient safety. As ONC/ASTP continues its modernization efforts, preserving these safety‑focused criteria will help ensure that progress in interoperability and innovation continues in parallel with a steadfast commitment to protecting patients. I appreciate the opportunity to offer feedback and thank ONC/ASTP for its continued dedication to improving the safety, reliability, and overall performance of health IT.

comment_text:
I understand that HHS has proposed a rule (HTI-5) that would eliminate more than half of the current certification requirements for electronic health record systems, removing federal guardrails before updating the standards that ensure medical records are reliable. That puts the cart before the horse. Audit trails are foundational to patient safety, fraud prevention, and accountability in litigation. Standards must be modernized—especially to address AI-generated content, interoperability, metadata transparency, and data retention—before certification protections are reduced. 

Mark Smith
Cincinnati, OH
