title: Comment from 1upHealth, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
1upHealth supports family history, reconciliation, patient health information capture and care plans as either well-embedded or not meeting needs. Audit support, safety-enhanced and accessibility centered features should be decided by markets. 
Security and privacy are critical. ASTP should defer to the Internet’s more advanced security and privacy and to OCR. Removal of authentication, access controls, disclosure audits and access audits will reduce conflict with OCR. 
Quality measurement needs change. Bespoke EMR APIs should give way to FHIR APIs. ASTP and CMS should phase out HEDIS payments based on data obtained from chart pulls or hidden data such as non-transparent and anti-competitive payer-provider platforms. CMS could require quality data include metadata from the APIs used. ASTP should stop non-standards-based quality measures.
Please focus on APIs for a modern interconnected digital economy rather than today's episodic care. HTN, DM, and obesity require continuous care and this requires highly functioning APIs. We support removal of obsolete interop features such as VDT and changes to public health ECR which is inefficient and underspecified. 
The three targeted Information Blocking exceptions should be removed. They confuse and serve to information block without a corresponding public benefit. 
EHR write capabilities are valuable. While read capabilities can be readily externalized as with ODBC, write capabilities need more engineering because it is not clear how written data fields interact with EHR internals. ASTP should work with HL7 to standardize write data fields. Denial of Service attacks were raised as a reason to not allow APIs. ASTP should evaluate how to address this risk with measures to ensure cryptographic patient consent.
Manner Exhausted is confusing and should be eliminated with the understanding that vendors provide performant APIs.
The TEFCA exception should be eliminated as TEFCA is an impediment to RESTful API interoperability. Information Blocking is interfering with the access, exchange and use of health data. TEFCA’s QHIN brokers interfere with access, TEFCA’s ancient protocols interfere with exchange and TEFCA’s document centric inability to use individual data fields interferes with use. The evolution from the document page-view architecture of the 1990s has been based on the computability of individual data fields and RESTful approaches to interoperability. Every major consumer app uses RESTful. Current TEFCA however uses 1990s IHE document protocols so this exemption creates an island of document-only healthcare information effectively non-computable for modern apps. While Sequoia states they will port to FHIR they miss the inextricable relationship between RESTful protocols and the FHIR format. FHIR is of limited value with cumbersome IHE protocols. TEFCA should provide a clear readily computable implementation guide and real-world examples from more than one EHR using FHIR. Has TEFCA been able to do any true FHIR interoperability-one vendor has indirectly provided FHIR data but with an additional proxy process.
IHE is antiquated so there are few programmers limiting it to incumbents. We need competition based on computing on the data not just sharing documents. 

Non-standard security and privacy make TEFCA susceptible to breaches. QHIN brokers and their costs are concerning as they don’t fundamentally solve the problem of discovering where the patient data lives. It should be noted that anyone using TEFCA still has to formulate either directed queries or localizing queries since every EHR cannot be searched for every patient. While TEFCA payloads can be encrypted, queries and record location services violate patient privacy when generated by abortion, drug treatment and behavioral health clinics where the nature of the provider discloses sensitive patient information. TEFCA fails basic zero trust security principles and there have been multiple wholesale breaches of privacy. ASTP should get an independent security assessment to evaluate TEFCA’s just trust me permitted uses security.
TEFCA needs transparency. We don’t know how TEFCA use numbers are generated, what data was exchanged and by which QHINs and whether their counterparties hinder competition. Given the anti-competitive nature of TEFCA this information is critical to understand the scope of TEFCA’s regulatory capture. The comparator for TEFCA’s IAS performance and barriers to digital health apps should be the number of RESTful API calls made by the non-healthcare part of the US economy.
ASTP can advance Bulk FHIR by providing SLA spec standards to augment Bulk FHIR. ASTP technical guidance on RPA and agentic AI performance and data access would help. Cures requires ALL data be available. The 2020 rule predated LLMs and asked that each data field have a vocabulary tag of the vendor’s choice. Please flesh out the opportunities to more richly enable AI here.

title: Comment from Reider, Jacob, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Summary here - with full comments in the attached PDF

-----

Eight Specific Recommendations 

1. Retain Safety-Enhanced Design Requirements (Criterion 170.315(g)(3) Safety-Enhanced Design)
Poorly designed EHRs continue to harm patients.This criterion was ONC's direct response to the IOM's finding that "poor usability is one of the single greatest threats to patient safety." It requires formal user-centered design processes and usability testing for safety-critical functions. Recommendation: Retain the Safety-Enhanced Design criterion in full. The IOM's findings have not been superseded. In fact, these findings are of greater importance since the 21st Century Cures Act subsequently carved EHRs out of the definition of “medical device” thereby removing safety oversight for EHRs. 

2. Retain Quality Management System Requirements (Criterion 170.315(g)(4) Quality Management System)
Systematic quality and risk management processes are foundational to safe health IT development. The IOM specifically recommended these processes. Since health IT systems do not fall under FDA regulatory purview, elimination of existing QMS requirements can be expected to lead to erosion of safe health IT systems as they are updated to meet evolving technology requirements and user needs.  Recommendation: Retain the QMS requirement to ensure vendors maintain systematic approaches to product safety.

3. Retain Audit Log and Tamper-Resistance Requirements (Criteria 170.315(d)(2),(d)(3),(d)(10))
Audit trails serve multiple safety functions: investigating errors, detecting fraud, supporting quality improvement, and enabling enforcement. The eClinicalWorks case was prosecuted in part because of audit log failures. Removing these requirements eliminates a proven enforcement mechanism. Recommendation: Retain audit log requirements. If consolidation is desired, consolidate into a single comprehensive audit criterion rather than eliminating the function entirely.

4. Retain Authentication and Access Control Requirements (Criteria 170.315(d)(1), (d)(13))
Wrong-patient errors often cause serious patient harm. Authentication and access controls are the first line of defense. While HIPAA requires security measures, certification provides verification that they are implemented. Recommendation: Retain authentication requirements, including multi-factor authentication, to prevent wrong-patient errors.

5. Retain Clinical Decision Support Source Attribute Requirements (Criteria: 170.315(b)(11))
The source attribute requirements, particularly funding source disclosure, are essential to enable continuous quality improvement by updating the evidence basis for clinical decision support, and to improve transparency of  potential pharmaceutical industry bias of clinical decision support. The Practice Fusion case demonstrated the real-world harm that could occur when CDS funding is not transparent. Recommendation: Retain all source attribute requirements, especially those requiring disclosure of who developed the intervention, who funded it, and the bibliographic basis for clinical recommendations.

6. Reject the "Widespread Adoption" Rationale
The argument that capabilities are widespread and therefore no longer need certification inverts cause and effect. No other federal safety agency has made this argument. ONC should not be the first. Recommendation: Explicitly reject and remove any reference to the "widespread adoption" rationale for removing safety requirements. Acknowledge that widespread adoption is the result of certification, not a reason to eliminate it.

7. Distinguish System/Tool Interoperability from Intrinsic Safety
PIQI and similar frameworks can validate data quality at exchange. They cannot validate internal product safety. Both matter. They are complementary, not substitutes. Recommendation: Maintain certification criteria that address internal product safety (usability, CDS integrity, audit logging) even as the program shifts focus towards interoperability standards.

8. Preserve Enforcement Mechanisms
The False Claims Act theory used in eClinicalWorks depends on certification requirements existing. Removing requirements eliminates this enforcement tool. Recommendation: Before removing any certification criterion, explicitly analyze whether removal eliminates enforcement mechanisms that have proven effective.

title: Comment from Morrow, Caroline, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
I am an independent healthcare technology consultant with professional experience in quality improvement, healthcare operations, and clinical technology implementation, and a member of the American Physical Therapy Association (APTA) AI Taskforce. My attached comment, which are of my own opinion based on my expertise, addresses the proposed removal of Decision Support Intervention source attributes (§170.315(b)(11)(iv)-(v)), Intervention Risk Management requirements (§170.315(b)(11)(vi)), and privacy/security certification criteria (§170.315(d)(2), (d)(3), (d)(10)).

I respectfully oppose full removal of these requirements and instead propose retaining a streamlined minimum viable transparency framework covering: core identity and intended use; training data representativeness and fairness processes; quantitative performance baselines (including AUROC, sensitivity/specificity, and threshold rationale); ongoing maintenance and drift monitoring documentation; risk management; and a new local adaptation addendum for health systems that fine-tune or retrain certified models. This compromise would reduce developer burden while preserving the documentation infrastructure that health systems need for responsible deployment, fairness evaluation, root cause analysis, and continuous quality improvement of clinical AI. The detailed comment with specific regulatory text references and compromise proposal is attached.

title: Comment on HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
I appreciate ONC/ASTP’s continued leadership in advancing interoperability, improving data liquidity, and modernizing the Health IT Certification Program through the HTI-5 proposed rule. Removing HTI-5 protections would weaken the certification program’s role as a safety assurance mechanism and reduce accountability for ensuring that health IT systems incorporate baseline safety safeguards.

Rather than removing these criteria, I respectfully recommend that ONC/ASTP maintain them in their current form or optimize and modernize them as needed to align with evolving technologies and care delivery models

Health IT plays a central role in care delivery, and its design, functionality, and safeguards have direct implications for patient safety. My own research, my work with real-world safety events, and federal enforcement actions have demonstrated that deficiencies in usability, authentication, clinical decision support transparency, and quality management can contribute to medication errors, wrong-patient events, failures in care transitions, and an inability to investigate and correct system-related harm. 

The Health IT Certification Program has served as a critical national safety floor, ensuring that baseline protections are present across products and vendors. Certification criteria requiring usability testing, audit logging, clinical decision support transparency, authentication, and systematic quality management were adopted specifically to mitigate known and documented patient safety risks. These criteria help ensure accountability, transparency, and systematic risk mitigation in systems that clinicians and patients rely upon every day.

Based on analysis of the HTI-5 proposed removals, the following criteria represent important patient safety protections:
Tier 1: Critical Safety Risk Criteria
170.315(b)(11) – Decision Support Interventions (source attribution and transparency)
170.315(g)(3) – Safety-Enhanced Design (usability testing and human factors processes)
170.315(b)(2) – Clinical Information Reconciliation and Incorporation
170.315(d)(2) – Auditable Events and Tamper-Resistance

Tier 2: High Safety Risk Criteria
170.315(d)(1) – Authentication, Access Control, and Authorization
170.315(g)(4) – Quality Management System
170.315(d)(10) – Auditing Actions on Health Information
170.315(b)(1) – Transitions of Care
170.315(d)(13) – Multi-Factor Authentication

Tier 3: Moderate Safety Concern Criteria
170.315(a)(9) – Clinical Decision Support
170.315(d)(3) – Audit Reports
170.315(d)(5) – Automatic Access Time-Out
170.315(d)(8) – Integrity

These criteria collectively address core safety domains, including usability, clinical workflow reliability, authentication and access control, clinical decision support transparency, and systematic quality management. 

Optimization could include streamlining testing processes, harmonizing with existing standards, or clarifying requirements—but eliminating these criteria entirely risks removing foundational safeguards without evidence that the underlying safety risks have been eliminated or mitigated through superior alternative mechanisms.

I appreciate the opportunity to provide input and thank ONC/ASTP for its continued commitment to improving the safety and effectiveness of health IT.

title: Comment from Oliveira, Eliel, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Submission Pillar 1: Domestic Sovereignty for Critical Infrastructure "As HTI-5 eliminates 70% of prescriptive certification, I recommend that ASTP establish a 'Domestic Ownership Standard' for all Jurisdictional Hubs receiving federal infrastructure funds. To safeguard national security and align with state-level protections like the Lone Star Infrastructure laws, entities managing the primary data 'pipe' for a state should be 100% U.S.-owned and operated without collaboration with a foreign company. > Furthermore, in alignment with EO 14192 (Unleashing Prosperity through Deregulation) and the Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure Act (CIRCIA), ASTP should establish a 'Domestic-Control Standard' for entities serving as Jurisdictional Hubs. As we move toward AI-enabled interoperability, the entities managing the 'Data Pipe' for state-wide exchange must be U.S.-owned and operated to prevent foreign-parent interference in critical health infrastructure."

Pillar 2: The HDU as the "Manner Exception" Safety Net "I support the removal of the TEFCA Manner Exception but recommend that ASTP replace it with a 'State-Designated Utility (HDU) Safe Harbor.' Small rural hospitals cannot manage the technical risk of autonomous AI (as defined in HTI-5) on their own. I recommend that ASTP explicitly recognize State-Designated Health Data Utilities (HDUs) as the primary facilitators of the 'TEFCA Manner Exception' for Information Blocking. Designating an HDU provides these providers with a 'Technical Safe Harbor,' ensuring federal compliance while reducing administrative burden."

Pillar 3: Protecting Federal Grants (RHTP Alignment) "I recommend that any state receiving Rural Health Transformation Program (RHTP) grants utilize a 'CMS-Aligned Network' model. This prevents the fragmentation of data and ensures that federal funds are spent on public-interest utilities rather than proprietary, foreign-controlled silos. As HTI-5 expands the scope of 'Access' to include autonomous AI systems, I recommend that such access be managed through Non-Profit Jurisdictional Utilities to ensure the data used for AI-enabled clinical efficiency is subject to public-interest governance."

These comments represent my individual expert perspective as a member of HITAC and a health technology leader, and do not necessarily represent the consensus view of the full committee or my peers.

title: Comment from Lorenzi, Virginia, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text: I am commenting personally as a patient and more importantly as a family caregiver.  While I see the benefit of the ability for a patient or caregiver to be able to use the threat of information blocking as a way to get compliance to a request for data, I think that very few patients or caregivers will go this route.  At the point you need to make this thread, your relationship with your provider is broken and you must be very brave and willing to take the risk of upsetting your provider.  Instead, the data should just be easily available, and the certification program, along with the CMS PI initiatives for hospitals and outpatient docs supports that.  I do not believe that vendors or providers will continue to offer and maintain the features that are no longer certified.  While FHIR is great, it is currently not available to solve existing programs, like getting structured data from your patient portal in CCDA format or pushing structured data electronically to another provider (this is often done today between vendors with CCDA and NHIN Direct).  Also, notification of admission to PCPS is often done using DIRECT as well.  Use of DIRECT today reduces the use of phones calls, faxes or data just not flowing   This was my experience working in the field.  I think that the cert removals should be replaced with attestation only or should have dates far enough out that a FHIR replacement can be in place and well adopted.  As a patient and caregiver, I want the ability to ask my provider about when AI was used and to know if that is an AI that is flawed, like a recall of a car part, or to come up with my own conclusions of its use.  Without the AI transparency requirements in EHRs how can I get this information?  It will be much harder since my providers may very well not have it - EHR vendors could keep it in a black box.  This should be part of the record I am allowed to obtain.  While I appreciate AI I have a healthy skepticism to its accuracy either of the model or of its use.  Plus if it is useful that is good information as well.  Finally, I am disturbed by the removal of privacy and security criteria.  Again, a breach is too late.  I like the idea that the EHRs have these features and my providers have them turned on.  In addition, I like the idea that I can get a structured accounting of disclosures and audit trails if I find that it is needed.  In closing I do really really appreciate that the future FHIR forward direction includes bi-directional communication with patients.  While I am sad to see the PGHD criteria go, I would prefer to see structured FHIR based Writes, the ability for patients to schedule appointments, submit advance directives, electronically request amendments to their records and track the request through to resolution, and electronically request appeals to payors.  I also hope for a world where a patient can easily get truly have access to their own longitudinal record including the data they add in that they think is important and can interact with their providers, payors, registries, research, social services, and patient communities through their own personal health record.

title: Comment from EMR Discovery, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
Hi ASTP team,
I’m writing as a 35-year HIT industry person and as a patient, and I’m honestly worried about the direction of the HTI 5 proposal. I’m all for better interoperability, but changing certification rules before updating the basic standards that keep electronic health data safe feels backwards.
Audit trails are what make a medical record trustworthy, and the current standard is outdated. Healthcare has changed a ton since 2018—AI tools, device integrations, messaging systems, and more. If we loosen requirements now without modernizing how data is tracked and stored, we’re setting ourselves up for mistakes, safety issues, and even fraud.
We really need a new audit trail standard that:
•Shows what AI tools and devices did and when.
•Identifies which software was used.
•Captures detailed activity like edits, copy paste, and user roles.
•Makes sure patients can actually access their own records.
And we need clear rules about keeping data. Auto deleting messages or letting information disappear is dangerous. Data has to stick around in a readable format for as long as it’s needed for care, investigations, or legal reasons.
Before we change certification rules, we need updated definitions and updated data preservation standards. Otherwise, we’re removing guardrails without putting better ones in place.
Audit trails aren’t optional—they’re the foundation of trust and accountability. I’m asking HHS to pause the proposed changes until a stronger, modern standard is ready.
Thanks for reading,

title: Comment from Ullem, Beth Daley, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
We commend ONC/ASTP for its leadership in advancing interoperability and improving the usability and accessibility of health information through the HTI-5 proposed rule. Efforts to streamline certification requirements, promote standards-based exchange, and reduce unnecessary regulatory burden are important to supporting innovation and improving the efficiency of care delivery. Continued modernization of the certification program is essential as health IT evolves and becomes more deeply integrated into clinical decision-making.

At the same time, health IT is not simply an administrative tool—it is an integral component of clinical care, influencing diagnostic decisions, medication ordering, care coordination, and clinical documentation. When health IT systems lack appropriate safeguards, design rigor, or transparency, they can contribute to preventable harm. National reports, patient safety investigations, and peer-reviewed research have identified usability limitations, incomplete auditability, insufficient access controls, and opaque clinical decision support as contributing factors in medication errors, wrong-patient orders, delays in care, and barriers to identifying and correcting safety issues. The Health IT Certification Program has played a vital role in addressing these risks by establishing clear, enforceable expectations that vendors incorporate safety-focused design, accountability mechanisms, and systematic quality processes into their products.

Several of the certification criteria proposed for removal serve essential patient safety functions. These include requirements related to clinical decision support transparency, usability testing and safety-enhanced design, audit logging and audit report capabilities, authentication and access controls, multi-factor authentication, clinical information reconciliation, transitions of care, quality management systems, and protections to ensure data integrity. Specifically, the criteria identified in the HTI-5 analysis include:

Tier 1: Critical Safety Risk Criteria

170.315(b)(11) – Decision Support Interventions

170.315(g)(3) – Safety-Enhanced Design

170.315(b)(2) – Clinical Information Reconciliation and Incorporation

170.315(d)(2) – Auditable Events and Tamper-Resistance

Tier 2: High Safety Risk Criteria

170.315(d)(1) – Authentication, Access Control, and Authorization

170.315(g)(4) – Quality Management System

170.315(d)(10) – Auditing Actions on Health Information

170.315(b)(1) – Transitions of Care

170.315(d)(13) – Multi-Factor Authentication

Tier 3: Moderate Safety Concern Criteria

170.315(a)(9) – Clinical Decision Support

170.315(d)(3) – Audit Reports

170.315(d)(5) – Automatic Access Time-Out

170.315(d)(8) – Integrity

These requirements help ensure that health IT systems are developed and implemented with appropriate attention to usability, traceability, transparency, and risk management. They support clinicians’ ability to deliver safe care and enable healthcare organizations to investigate and address potential safety events when they occur. Importantly, certification provides a consistent, nationwide baseline that benefits healthcare organizations of all sizes, including those with limited technical resources to independently evaluate vendor systems.

We respectfully recommend that ONC/ASTP retain these safety-related criteria within the certification program or revise and strengthen them as appropriate to reflect current technology and best practices. While refinement and modernization may be warranted, removing these protections entirely would create unnecessary risk and reduce assurance that certified systems meet fundamental safety expectations.

The certification program has been instrumental in improving the reliability and safety of health IT nationwide. Preserving core safety-focused requirements will help ensure that progress in interoperability and innovation continues alongside a sustained commitment to protecting patients and supporting clinicians.

Thank you for your continued leadership and for the opportunity to comment on this important proposed rule.

- Beth Daley Ulem
- Blackford Middelton

title: Comment from Veradigm LLC, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
On behalf of Veradigm we thank the Office of the National Coordinator for the opportunity to provide comments on the Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: Certification Program Updates, Algorithm Transparency, and Information Sharing (HTI 5) proposed rule. We appreciate ONC’s continued commitment to advancing interoperability, reducing unnecessary regulatory burden, and modernizing the nation’s health information infrastructure.

We welcome the direction outlined in HTI 5, particularly ONC’s efforts to streamline certification, promote clarity in the information blocking regulations, and prepare the ecosystem for a more API centered interoperability framework. At the same time, we believe several aspects of the proposed rule warrant further consideration to ensure operational stability, alignment with CMS program requirements, and continued trust and usability for providers, patients, and public health agencies.

To help frame our detailed comments in the attached pdf, we have summarized below the overarching themes reflected across our feedback:

Key Themes of Our Comments

1.Support for Modernization with Structured, Predictable Transitions
We support ONC’s direction toward FHIR based interoperability but recommend careful, phased timelines—particularly where removal of C CDA, Direct, or standards based reporting could create gaps before replacement frameworks are fully implemented and adopted.

2.Avoiding Unintended Burden Through Premature Deregulation
While we appreciate ONC’s deregulatory intent, in several areas (e.g., transitions of care, reconciliation, real world testing changes, automated numerator recording), removing requirements without fully operational alternatives may inadvertently increase burden, complexity, or financial risk for developers and providers.

3.Need for Alignment with CMS Programs and Industry Dependencies
Many certification criteria remain tightly coupled to CMS Promoting Interoperability measures and real world clinical workflows. We encourage ONC to synchronize effective dates, retirement timelines, and expectations across programs to avoid misalignment, conflicting requirements, or operational disruption.

4.Preserving Transparency, Safety, and Trust—Especially for AI Enabled Tools
We support simplification of the Decision Support Intervention (DSI) criterion but highlight the importance of maintaining transparency expectations—particularly around provenance, accountability, and clinician oversight—given increasing reliance on predictive and automated technologies.

5.Recognition That Some Proposed Removals Do Not Reduce Practical Burden
In areas such as accessibility, demographics, device information, and security controls, the removal of certification criteria does not eliminate underlying legal or operational obligations. Our comments suggest clarity to avoid creating the appearance of burden reduction where real world responsibilities remain unchanged.

6.Support for Removing Low Value or Obsolete Criteria
We agree with ONC’s efforts to streamline the program by removing outdated, low utilization, or duplicative certification criteria, including SED, certain security requirements, unused public health survey criteria, and fully deprecated definitions.

7.Recommendations for Thoughtful Modernization of “Access, Exchange, and Use” Definitions

We support inclusion of automated technologies within these definitions, while recommending that ONC ensure consistency across access, use, and exchange and reinforce safeguards for patient matching, routing, and human in the loop review where clinically appropriate.

We appreciate ONC’s thoughtful approach in developing the HTI 5 proposed rule and remain fully supportive of the agency’s long term interoperability vision. We hope our feedback is helpful as ONC works to finalize a rule that promotes both innovation and stability across the health information ecosystem.

title: Comment from Dynamic Health IT, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
We feel that the proposed changes to the 170.315(b)(1) Transitions of Care (TOC) criterion and removal of the 170.315(b)(2) Clinical information reconciliation and incorporation criterion will be detrimental to healthcare interoperability.  ONC/ASTP mentions FHIR as an alternative but there is no current certification requirement to receive and ingest TOC FHIR data. The current C-CDA Validation Tool is important for ensuring useful, interoperable C-CDAs.  Even with the current (b)(1) requirements, we’ve seen many production C-CDAs that couldn’t pass the Validation Tool.  Without it, many EHR vendors will either:

- Stop generating C-CDAs entirely, or
- Generate C-CDAs with errors that prevent them from being ingested.

Without a replacement requirement for EHRs to support bi-directional FHIR data, this leaves a big gap in healthcare interoperability and the entire TOC process. FHIR Documents are the apparent replacement for the C-CDA and, although there is an HL7 Implementation Guide, there is no associated certification criterion to ensure adoption.

Population-based cancer surveillance provides unbiased, representative cancer statistics for monitoring cancer occurrence and trends across all populations in the US. It is the backbone for cancer control and public health policy and assessments. Every state in the US has a state law mandating cancer reporting that need to follow data reporting standards set by the North American Associations of Central Cancer Registries (NAACCR) that serves as the standard setter for North America, working collaboratively with the stakeholders and support from the cancer registry community. Standards are important to be consistently followed and updated in order to ensure the data quality and sound analyses. The HTI-5 proposed deregulation measures risk the derailment of the existing NAACCR standards. Therefore, I fully support the analysis and recommended revision as submitted by NAACCR.

title: Comment on HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
First, I do appreciate the proposal’s purpose of updating current regulations around health data, technology, and interoperability. The rapid AI adoption in healthcare warrants regulations that encourages healthy competitive markets while maintaining the integrity and safety of a patient’s individual health care. 
However, I implore you to revisit the proposal’s plan for Certification Criteria for Health Information Technology. Of the current 60 criteria, 34 are being removed and 7 revised. What poses the greatest threat to the integrity and safety of health care data are: 
the removal of: 
§ 170.315(d)(1): Authentication, access control, authorization 
§ 170.315(d)(2): Auditable events and tamper-resistance 
§ 170.315(d)(3): Audit report(s) 
§ 170.315(d)(4): Amendments
§ 170.315(d)(5): Automatic access time-out 
§ 170.315(d)(6): Emergency access 
§ 170.315(d)(7): End-user device encryption 
§ 170.315(d)(8): Integrity 
§ 170.315(d)(9): Trusted connection 
§ 170.315(d)(12): Encrypt authentication credentials 
§ 170.315(d)(13): Multi-factor authentication (MFA)
and the revision of: 
§ 170.315(b)(11): Decision Support Interventions (DSI)
The ASTP/ONC justifies the removals and revisions by stating they are “redundant” of the HIPAA Security Rule. This argument ultimately fails to account for the functional difference between product-level certification and organizational-level compliance. By removing the 11 security criteria, the ASTP/ONC effectively shifts the technical vetting burden from a federal testing process to the health care providers/organizations This is not a net reduction in industry burden. It now becomes a massive unfunded mandate for individual health care organizations to perform manual, product-specific technical audits that were previously covered by the “Certified Health IT” certification. 
The revision of § 170.315(b)(11) will inevitably trigger a significant regression in clinical safety. Removing the “Model Card” deletes the 31 attributes that are required for developers to share. This would mean clinicians will no longer see how the AI was built or tested. In the eyes of health risk management, removal of the label no longer guarantees that the AI-model has been vetted. Health organizations will now inherit 100% of the risk. 
This proposal ultimately shifts the burden of risk, integrity, and patient safety wholly to providers and health organizations. It correctly identifies the cost of regulation to developers but it fails to consider for the multiplied costs to the healthcare delivery system. At its core, this proposal is a systemic transfer of liability.
